class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Operations Management and Productivity
learning_objectives: Define operations management and distinguish between goods-producing and service-producing operations
Explain the role of operations as one of the three core business functions alongside marketing and finance
Calculate single-factor and multi-factor productivity and interpret productivity trends
Identify historical milestones in operations management from Taylor's scientific management through Industry 4.0
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management: Sustainability and Supply Chain Management (13th ed.), Ch. 1
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 1
Skinner, 'Manufacturing - Missing Link in Corporate Strategy,' Harvard Business Review (1969)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every product you touch and every service you experience exists because someone designed and managed an operations system to produce it. Amazon ships over 1.6 million packages daily from fulfillment centers that use robotic retrieval systems, predictive algorithms, and carefully choreographed human labor. Toyota builds a Camry every 55 seconds at its Georgetown, Kentucky plant using the Toyota Production System that became the blueprint for modern lean manufacturing. Starbucks serves roughly 100 million customer visits per week across 35,000 stores with standardized recipes, equipment, and service protocols. Behind each of these achievements is operations management, the discipline concerned with designing, operating, and improving the systems that create and deliver a company's primary products and services. Operations is where strategy meets reality: it is the function that actually makes the promises the rest of the company sells. When operations works well, customers see consistent quality, fair prices, and on-time delivery. When it fails, customers see recalls, stockouts, Boeing 737 MAX groundings, and Theranos-scale debacles. This course develops your ability to analyze operational systems quantitatively and make decisions about process design, capacity, inventory, quality, and supply chain that determine whether a business is profitable or unprofitable, whether customers are satisfied or frustrated, and whether a strategy can actually be executed.
key_concepts: Operations management definition: the set of activities that creates value in the form of goods and services by transforming inputs into outputs; includes process design, capacity, layout, scheduling, quality, inventory, and supply chain

Three core business functions: marketing (generates demand), finance (tracks how well the organization is doing and raises capital), and operations (creates the product or service) - each depends on the others for the firm to succeed

Goods vs. services differences: tangibility, inventoriability, customer contact, response time, labor content, and quality measurement each differ, but most modern offerings are actually a bundle of goods and services (e.g., a smartphone plus app ecosystem plus warranty service)

Productivity = outputs / inputs; single-factor productivity (units per labor hour) is easy to measure but ignores capital and materials; multi-factor productivity (output / (labor + capital + materials + energy)) gives a more complete picture

Productivity growth is the engine of living standards: the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reports nonfarm business productivity grew roughly 2.1% annually from 1947-2023, but has slowed markedly since 2007; operations is where that growth is earned

Scientific management (Taylor, 1911): time and motion study, standardization, specialization, and scientific selection of workers; transformed industrial productivity but criticized for treating workers as machines

Statistical quality control (Shewhart, Deming): moved quality from inspection to prevention through control charts and process capability analysis; Deming's teachings transformed post-war Japanese industry

Lean and Toyota Production System (Ohno, 1950s-1970s): just-in-time production, jidoka (automation with a human touch), and continuous elimination of waste became the global standard for efficient manufacturing
Industry 4.0 (2010s-present): cyber-physical systems, industrial IoT, predictive analytics, digital twins, and additive manufacturing represent the current frontier of operations innovation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Productivity analysis of two competing bakeries. Bakery A produces 1,500 loaves per day using 6 workers (8 hours each) and $400 in materials and energy. Bakery B produces 2,200 loaves per day using 10 workers (8 hours each) and $550 in materials and energy. Compute labor productivity (loaves per labor hour) for each: A = 1500/48 = 31.25, B = 2200/80 = 27.5. By this measure A is more productive. Now compute multifactor productivity using wages at $18/hour and combining labor and materials: A = 1500 / ($864 + $400) = 1.186 loaves per dollar; B = 2200 / ($1440 + $550) = 1.106. A remains more productive. Discuss when each measure is appropriate and what it misses (e.g., quality differences, capital investment in ovens). Connect to real productivity debates such as why the retail sector appears to have higher labor productivity in the US than in France (Walmart effect).
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet tool for productivity calculations (Excel or Google Sheets)
Access to Bureau of Labor Statistics productivity datasets
Case study packet: 'How Toyota Builds the Best Cars in the World'
Calculator or computational environment

review.value: Strategy without operations capability is wishful thinking; Walmart's everyday low prices strategy is meaningless without operations that actually achieve the lowest cost per case delivered, and Zara's fast fashion strategy collapses without a supply chain that can replenish stores in two weeks

A firm that boosts labor productivity by investing heavily in automation may see multifactor productivity fall if the capital cost exceeds the labor savings; always check whether the measurement frame captures the real trade-offs

The customer-facing nature of services does not exempt them from operations analysis; hospitals, airlines, and hotels use the same tools (forecasting, capacity planning, quality control) with adaptations for intangibility and perishability of inventory

Today's debates about AI-driven automation echo earlier debates about Taylorism, robotics, and ERP systems; knowing how prior waves of operational innovation affected productivity, jobs, and competitive advantage sharpens judgment about the current wave

learning_objectives: Define the four competitive priorities (cost, quality, delivery, flexibility) and explain the order-winner / order-qualifier distinction
Analyze the alignment between business strategy and operations strategy using the Hayes-Wheelwright framework
Identify structural and infrastructural decisions in operations strategy
Evaluate how a firm's operations capabilities constrain or enable its chosen competitive position
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 2
Hayes & Wheelwright, Restoring Our Competitive Edge (1984)
Hill, Manufacturing Strategy: Text and Cases (3rd ed.), Ch. 2-3
Porter, 'What is Strategy?' Harvard Business Review (1996)

lecture_notes.introduction: A firm cannot be the best at everything. Southwest Airlines cannot match Singapore Airlines' in-flight luxury because Southwest's entire operations system (single aircraft type, 25-minute turnarounds, no assigned seating, no meals) is engineered for low cost and high aircraft utilization. Singapore Airlines cannot match Southwest's fares because its operations (premium catering, lie-flat seats, trained cabin crew) are engineered for a completely different outcome. Operations strategy is the discipline of deciding which trade-offs a firm will embrace so that its operations system becomes a source of sustainable advantage rather than a patchwork of compromises that pleases no one. The concepts in this session frame the rest of the course: every process choice, every inventory policy, every quality standard, and every supply chain decision should be evaluated against how well it advances the firm's chosen competitive priorities. Terry Hill's distinction between order-qualifiers and order-winners is essential: some performance dimensions must merely be good enough for customers to consider you at all, while others are what actually tip the purchase decision. A firm that invests in order-winners wins business; a firm that over-invests in order-qualifiers wastes money. The cost of getting operations strategy wrong is visible at JCPenney, whose 2012 pricing and store operations overhaul alienated its core customers, or at Boeing, whose cost-focused 737 MAX development and outsourcing strategy collided with the quality imperatives of passenger aviation.
key_concepts: Competitive priorities: cost (low price through low-cost operations), quality (conformance and performance), delivery (speed and reliability), and flexibility (volume, product mix, and new product introduction)

Order-qualifiers vs. order-winners: qualifiers are minimum thresholds to be considered by the customer (e.g., safety in aviation, cleanliness in food service); order-winners actually decide the purchase among qualified alternatives
Structural decisions: capacity, facilities (number, size, location), technology, and vertical integration; hard to reverse, long lead times, multi-year impact
Infrastructural decisions: workforce policies, quality systems, planning and control systems, organization structure, and performance measurement; easier to change but equally important to alignment

Hayes-Wheelwright four stages of operations contribution: internally neutral (minimize negatives), externally neutral (match competitors), internally supportive (credibly support strategy), and externally supportive (operations-based competitive advantage)

Trade-offs and the sand cone model: Skinner argued trade-offs are unavoidable, while Ferdows and De Meyer's sand cone suggested capabilities build on each other in a specific order (quality, dependability, flexibility, cost)

Operations strategy must support the generic strategy: cost leadership requires low-cost operations; differentiation requires superior quality, features, or service; focus requires operations tuned to the target segment's needs

Product-process matrix: different product volumes and varieties demand different process types (project, job shop, batch, assembly line, continuous flow); misalignment creates the 'wrong' factory for the product
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the order-winner framework to three real competitors in the coffee market: Starbucks, Dunkin', and McDonald's McCafe. Identify each firm's qualifiers (e.g., coffee must be hot and safe) and order-winners (Starbucks: customization, experience, and brand; Dunkin': speed and convenience; McDonald's: low price and drive-thru throughput). Map each to the implied operations choices: Starbucks invests in barista training, espresso machines, and store ambiance; Dunkin' invests in queue design and standardized drink prep; McDonald's leverages its existing drive-thru infrastructure and high-volume equipment. Conclude by evaluating an operational decision (e.g., Starbucks adding mobile order-ahead) in terms of whether it strengthens or weakens the firm's order-winners.
setup_requirements: Case study packet: 'Southwest Airlines: In a Different World' (HBS 9-910-419)
Hayes-Wheelwright framework reference
Porter's generic strategies reference
Access to annual reports for case companies

review.value: An operations system that is excellent in some absolute sense but misaligned with the firm's strategy is worse than a mediocre but aligned system; operations strategy is about fit, not universal excellence

Spending to improve a qualifier past the threshold earns nothing; spending to improve an order-winner can shift purchase decisions; knowing which is which is the most valuable question in operations strategy
Once a firm has invested in a network of small dispersed plants it cannot easily become a cost leader; structural choices constrain strategic flexibility for years and must be made deliberately

Despite decades of 'have it all' rhetoric, firms that try to be simultaneously the lowest cost, highest quality, fastest, and most flexible usually achieve none of these; discipline about priorities separates winners from strugglers

learning_objectives: Apply time series forecasting methods: naive, moving average, weighted moving average, and exponential smoothing
Decompose a time series into trend, seasonality, cyclic, and random components
Compute and interpret forecast error measures: MAD, MSE, MAPE, and tracking signal
Select an appropriate forecasting method given data characteristics and business context
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 4
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 18
Makridakis, Wheelwright & Hyndman, Forecasting: Methods and Applications (3rd ed.), Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Every operational decision depends on a forecast. A hospital buying ventilators is guessing how many COVID patients it will treat. Walmart ordering Thanksgiving turkeys is predicting how many families will buy them. Tesla setting Gigafactory production is betting on battery demand 18 months out. Each wrong forecast costs money: too high and inventory piles up, capital is wasted, and markdowns destroy margins (the retail industry routinely takes billions in inventory write-downs from over-forecasting); too low and customers see stockouts, sales are lost, and expensive expediting is needed (Apple's iPhone launches have repeatedly been constrained by under-forecasting critical components). Forecasting is not fortune-telling, and no method can eliminate error, but the right method applied with discipline reduces error enough to make the difference between profit and loss. This session develops the toolkit of quantitative time series forecasting, teaches you to measure accuracy rigorously, and frames the judgment questions about when to rely on numerical models vs. managerial judgment. Zara's remarkable forecasting approach combines point-of-sale data, local store manager input, and rapid production response to keep forecast horizons short enough that errors never grow large; the lesson is that good forecasting is partly about the math and partly about operational design that reduces the forecasting burden.
key_concepts: Time series components: level (baseline average), trend (long-term movement up or down), seasonality (regular within-year patterns), cyclic (multi-year business cycles), and random noise; separating these is the first step in choosing a model
Naive forecast: F(t+1) = A(t), the current period's actual becomes next period's forecast; surprisingly hard to beat when data has no strong pattern, and a useful baseline
Moving average of n periods: F(t+1) = average of last n actuals; smooths random noise but lags trend and seasonality; longer n means more smoothing but more lag
Weighted moving average: assigns larger weights to more recent data; weights sum to 1.0; responds faster to changes but still lags trend
Exponential smoothing: F(t+1) = alpha * A(t) + (1-alpha) * F(t); alpha in (0,1) controls responsiveness; easy to compute with minimal data storage, the workhorse of operational forecasting
Trend-adjusted (Holt's) exponential smoothing: separately smooths level (alpha) and trend (beta); appropriate when data has a persistent trend component
Winter's method: extends Holt's to handle seasonality with a third smoothing constant (gamma); widely used for monthly retail sales and similar seasonal patterns

Forecast error measures: MAD (mean absolute deviation, in original units), MSE (mean squared error, penalizes large errors), MAPE (mean absolute percentage error, scale-free and comparable across products)
Tracking signal = running sum of forecast errors / MAD; when it exceeds plus or minus 4 MAD, the forecast is biased and the model should be re-examined
Causal (regression) models relate demand to independent variables (price, promotion, weather, holidays); appropriate when historical data alone misses important drivers of demand
lecture_notes.demonstration: Forecast January sales for a regional ice cream distributor. Monthly sales in thousands of units for the past year: Jan 42, Feb 45, Mar 60, Apr 85, May 120, Jun 160, Jul 175, Aug 170, Sep 130, Oct 90, Nov 55, Dec 48. Apply three methods: 3-month moving average for Jan = (48 + 55 + 90)/3 = 64.3. Clearly wrong - the data is strongly seasonal and last year's January was 42. Apply exponential smoothing with alpha = 0.3 starting from F(Jan) = 42: forecasts drift upward through summer, badly overshooting Dec. Apply Winter's method or a seasonal index approach: compute monthly seasonal index by dividing each month by the annual average, then forecast next January = trend-adjusted level times seasonal index(Jan) ~ 43-45. Compare MAPE for each method and show why the seasonal model dominates. Discuss how misreading seasonality creates massive forecasting errors in retail and food service.
setup_requirements: Excel with Data Analysis Toolpak or Python/Pandas
Historical sales dataset (provided) with 36+ months of data
Forecast error computation template
Case packet: 'Forecasting at Zara'

review.key: Match the Method to the Data
review.value: Using exponential smoothing on strongly seasonal data without a seasonal component is a common error; always plot the data first to see what pattern you are trying to capture before choosing a model
MAPE allows comparison across products with different volumes; a MAD of 10 is huge for a product selling 20 units and trivial for one selling 2,000, so using MAD alone misleads

Random errors should cancel out over time; a tracking signal that drifts outside plus/minus 4 indicates the model is systematically too high or too low, which is a signal to retrain or rebuild the model

Zara's fast supply chain allows it to forecast two weeks rather than six months ahead; shortening horizon reduces error regardless of method, and is often a more valuable operational investment than a better algorithm

learning_objectives: Define design capacity, effective capacity, and utilization, and compute efficiency and capacity cushion
Apply break-even analysis to capacity decisions with fixed and variable costs
Use decision trees with expected monetary value to choose among capacity alternatives under uncertainty
Explain the strategic implications of capacity leads, lags, and average strategies
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 7 and S7
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 5
Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (6th ed.), Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: Capacity decisions are among the most consequential choices a firm makes. They require millions to billions of dollars, cannot be reversed quickly, lock in cost structures for years, and determine whether the firm can satisfy demand when it comes. TSMC's decision to build a $40 billion Arizona semiconductor fab is a capacity decision whose wisdom will only become clear over a decade. Intel's 2013 decision to delay 10nm investment cost the firm its manufacturing leadership to TSMC and Samsung and required a catastrophic strategic reset a decade later. Amazon's years of over-building warehouse capacity during the pandemic led to 2022 layoffs and closure announcements when demand growth normalized. The fundamental challenge is that capacity must be decided before demand is known, and both excess capacity (wasted fixed costs, dragged profitability) and insufficient capacity (lost sales, expensive expediting, dissatisfied customers) are expensive. This session introduces the quantitative toolkit for capacity decisions: break-even analysis for simple cost-volume trade-offs, expected value decision trees for uncertain demand, and strategic frameworks for the timing and sizing of capacity additions. The tools matter, but the judgment matters more, because the inputs to the models (probabilities, market growth, competitor response) are themselves uncertain.
key_concepts: Design capacity: maximum theoretical output per unit time under ideal conditions; rarely achieved in practice
Effective capacity: maximum output given realistic product mix, scheduling, maintenance, and quality requirements; always less than design capacity
Utilization = actual output / design capacity; efficiency = actual output / effective capacity; these measure different things and should not be confused

Capacity cushion = 100% minus utilization, expressed as a percentage; high-variance demand industries (hospitals, consulting firms) need large cushions, stable predictable operations can run with minimal cushion
Break-even analysis: at break-even volume Q, total revenue = total cost, so PQ = F + VQ, giving Q = F / (P - V); useful for comparing capacity alternatives with different fixed/variable cost mixes
Decision trees: graph decision nodes (squares) and chance nodes (circles); compute expected monetary value by rolling back from terminal payoffs, weighting each branch by its probability
Economies of scale: unit cost typically falls as plant size increases because fixed costs are spread over more units and large equipment is more efficient per unit of output
Diseconomies of scale: beyond some size, unit costs rise due to communication overhead, increased complexity, and management span-of-control limits; there is an optimal plant size for each industry
Capacity leading (build ahead of demand): captures market share, ensures availability, but risks excess capacity if demand is lower than expected; used when opportunity cost of stockouts is high

Capacity lagging (build after demand): minimizes risk of overbuilding, improves capacity utilization and cost, but risks stockouts and lost customers; used in mature markets where growth is predictable
Capacity averaging: build capacity matching average expected demand; accepts alternating over- and under-supply, middle-ground approach
lecture_notes.demonstration: A food processor is deciding between building a small plant ($4M fixed annual cost, $12/unit variable cost) or a large plant ($7M fixed annual cost, $8/unit variable cost). Selling price is $18/unit. Compute break-even: small plant Q = 4M/(18-12) = 666,667 units; large plant Q = 7M/(18-8) = 700,000 units. Compute indifference point between plants: 4M + 12Q = 7M + 8Q, so 4Q = 3M, so Q = 750,000 units. Below 750K choose small, above 750K choose large. Now add uncertainty: demand will be either 500K (probability 0.3), 800K (probability 0.5), or 1.1M (probability 0.2). Build a decision tree: compute expected profit for each plant under each demand level, roll back to get EMV(small) = $1.8M, EMV(large) = $2.9M. Large plant has higher EMV but higher downside risk; discuss risk attitude and when EMV is the wrong criterion.
setup_requirements: Decision tree software (TreePlan for Excel, or SilverDecisions) or whiteboard
Cost data for case analysis
Break-even calculator template
Case packet: 'Tesla Gigafactory Capacity Decisions'

review.value: Unlike most operational decisions, capacity cannot be changed next quarter; it takes 18-36 months to build a factory and years to close one gracefully, so capacity decisions must account for long horizons even when near-term pressures dominate

Benchmarks against design capacity hide operational problems; the gap between design and effective capacity is where maintenance, quality losses, and product mix constraints live, and shrinking that gap is often cheaper than adding new capacity

review.key: EMV Is a Starting Point, Not an Answer
review.value: Decision trees with expected value assume risk neutrality, which is appropriate when firms can diversify across many such decisions; for bet-the-company capacity commitments, risk-adjusted analysis and downside scenarios matter more than simple EMV

review.key: Lead, Lag, or Match Reflects Strategy
review.value: A firm pursuing market share and customer-experience differentiation leads demand with capacity; a firm pursuing cost leadership and high utilization lags demand; the choice should flow from competitive priorities, not from a capacity model in isolation

learning_objectives: Classify processes along the product-process matrix: project, job shop, batch, repetitive (assembly line), and continuous flow
Construct a process flowchart and identify bottlenecks, cycle time, and throughput
Apply Little's Law (WIP = throughput times flow time) to analyze work-in-process and lead time
Design process-oriented and product-oriented layouts and compute line balancing for assembly lines
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 7 and Ch. 9
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 7-8
Hopp & Spearman, Factory Physics (3rd ed.), Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: Every product is produced by a process, and the choice of process determines cost, speed, quality, and flexibility before any improvement effort can begin. A custom yacht builder uses a project layout in which workers and materials come to the stationary product. A hospital emergency department uses a job shop layout with functional departments (triage, imaging, surgery, pharmacy) through which patients route based on their individual needs. A Coca-Cola bottling line uses a repetitive assembly line where every unit follows the same sequence. An oil refinery uses continuous flow, where material moves through pipes without ever stopping. None of these is inherently better; each matches a different combination of product variety and volume. The product-process matrix, introduced by Hayes and Wheelwright, makes this alignment explicit and explains why mismatches are costly. Alongside this strategic framing, process analysis provides a quantitative toolkit for understanding any process: flowcharts reveal steps, bottlenecks constrain throughput, and Little's Law links flow time, WIP, and throughput in a relationship so universal it applies equally to a hospital ER and an Amazon fulfillment center. Process thinking is the backbone of operational improvement. The Toyota Production System's genius was not new technology but an obsessive attention to process: observing, measuring, and continuously improving every step.
key_concepts: Project process: one-of-a-kind product with high customization, low volume, long duration (e.g., construction, major consulting engagement); resources move to the product
Job shop: low volume, high variety, functional layout (machine shop, hospital, general contractor); each job follows a different route
Batch: moderate volume, moderate variety; products made in groups moving through similar but not identical routes (bakery, paint manufacturing, small-run electronics)
Repetitive / assembly line: high volume, low variety, product-oriented layout with balanced sequential stations (auto assembly, appliance manufacturing, fast food)
Continuous flow: very high volume, very low variety, process-oriented with 24/7 operation (refinery, paper mill, chemical plant)
Cycle time (takt time in lean language): time between completions of consecutive units; = 1 / throughput rate; determined by the slowest (bottleneck) station
Throughput = units completed per unit time; maximum throughput = 1 / bottleneck cycle time
Flow time (lead time, throughput time): elapsed time from start to finish for one unit; includes processing, waiting, moving, and inspection

Little's Law: WIP = throughput times flow time; universal and unconditional, allows computing any one variable from the other two (e.g., if throughput is 50/hour and flow time is 4 hours, WIP averages 200)
Bottleneck: the resource or station with the highest utilization; determines maximum throughput; improving non-bottleneck resources does not increase throughput
Process-oriented (functional) layout: similar machines grouped together; flexible for job shop and batch; high material handling costs and long lead times
Product-oriented (line) layout: workstations arranged in sequence of operations; efficient for high volume; vulnerable to breakdowns and inflexible to product changes
Line balancing: assigning tasks to workstations to minimize idle time given a required takt time; balance delay = (n times cycle time - sum of task times) / (n times cycle time)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Balance an assembly line for electric toothbrushes. Tasks with precedence: A(40s), B(30s after A), C(50s after A), D(40s after B), E(60s after C), F(25s after D and E), G(35s after F). Total task time = 280 seconds. Required output = 50 units per hour, so takt time = 3600/50 = 72 seconds. Minimum number of workstations = ceiling(280/72) = 4. Assign tasks using longest operation time rule: Station 1 = A+B (70s), Station 2 = C (50s), Station 3 = D+E (100s violates 72s), so redo. Work through precedence constraints carefully, ending with a 4 or 5 station configuration, compute balance delay, and discuss the relationship between takt time and staffing.
setup_requirements: Flowcharting software (Lucidchart, Visio, draw.io)
Line balancing spreadsheet template
Case packet: 'The Toyota Production System' and 'Process Analysis at Starbucks'
Simulation software (SimQuick or Arena) for bottleneck analysis

review.value: Non-bottleneck improvements produce no throughput benefit and may make things worse by adding WIP; Goldratt's Theory of Constraints is built entirely on this insight and has transformed dozens of factories by focusing first on the constraint

WIP = throughput times flow time holds for any stable process, from an emergency room to an Amazon warehouse; using it in reverse lets you ask 'given this target flow time and throughput, how much WIP is acceptable?'

High variety plus low volume demands process layout; low variety plus high volume demands product layout; firms that try to run one type of product through the wrong layout pay cost and quality penalties
A line with 40% balance delay has effectively 40% idle capacity; improving balance by redistributing tasks is the cheapest source of throughput improvement before considering new equipment or workers

class_schedule.topic: Quality Management, TQM, and Statistical Process Control
learning_objectives: Define quality from multiple perspectives (conformance, fitness for use, value, transcendent) and explain the cost of quality
Describe the principles of Total Quality Management and the contributions of Deming, Juran, and Crosby
Construct and interpret control charts for variables (X-bar, R) and attributes (p, c)
Compute process capability indices Cp and Cpk and explain their role in manufacturing
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 6 and S6
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 12-13
Deming, Out of the Crisis (1986), Ch. 1-3
Juran, Juran on Quality by Design

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality is the operational dimension most directly tied to brand, trust, and survival. The cost of poor quality includes not just scrap and rework but customer defection, warranty claims, regulatory penalties, and in the worst cases, catastrophic loss of life. Boeing's 737 MAX quality failures killed 346 people and cost the firm over $20 billion in direct costs, plus immeasurable reputation damage that has not fully recovered. Toyota's 2009-2010 unintended acceleration recalls cost $2.4 billion and permanently dented its quality reputation. In pharmaceuticals, Johnson & Johnson's Tylenol response in 1982 became a textbook example of how quality commitment preserves brand, while Theranos is a reminder that faking quality is worse than having none. Quality is fundamentally a systems property: it emerges from the design of processes, the training of workers, the selection of suppliers, and the culture of the organization. This session covers both the philosophy of quality management (the ideas of Deming, Juran, and others that transformed Japanese and then global manufacturing) and the statistical toolkit of process control that lets you detect quality problems early, before defective product reaches customers. Understanding statistical process control separates firms that build quality in from firms that inspect it in, and the operational and cost differences are enormous.
key_concepts: Definitions of quality: transcendent (you know it when you see it), product-based (measurable attributes), user-based (fitness for use), manufacturing-based (conformance to specifications), value-based (quality per unit cost)

Cost of quality (COQ): prevention (training, design reviews), appraisal (inspection, testing), internal failure (scrap, rework), external failure (warranty, recalls, lost customers); 1-10-100 rule: $1 in prevention saves $10 in appraisal saves $100 in failure

Deming's 14 points: constancy of purpose, cease dependence on inspection, long-term supplier relationships, drive out fear, break down barriers between departments, among others; emphasized management responsibility and continuous improvement
Juran's quality trilogy: quality planning, quality control, quality improvement; Juran argued 80% of quality problems are management-controllable (the Pareto principle applied to quality)

Crosby's four absolutes: quality is conformance to requirements, prevention not inspection, zero defects standard, measure cost of quality in dollars; popularized 'quality is free' because failure costs exceed prevention costs
Total Quality Management (TQM): customer focus, continuous improvement, employee empowerment, benchmarking, supplier partnerships, and process approach; foundation for Six Sigma and Lean

Statistical Process Control (SPC): use statistics to distinguish common cause variation (inherent to the process) from special cause variation (assignable to specific events); control charts signal when to intervene
X-bar and R charts for variables data (continuous measurements); control limits at plus/minus 3 standard errors; rules for detecting out-of-control signals (points beyond limits, runs, trends)
p-charts for fraction defective, c-charts for count of defects per unit; use when measurements are counts rather than continuous variables

Process capability: Cp = (USL - LSL) / (6 sigma) measures potential capability (width of specs vs. process variation); Cpk = min[(USL - mean)/3sigma, (mean - LSL)/3sigma] accounts for process centering
Six Sigma standard: Cpk at least 2, corresponding to 3.4 defects per million opportunities assuming 1.5 sigma shift; Motorola invented it, GE and many others institutionalized it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze an X-bar and R chart for a bottle-filling operation. Target fill = 500mL, specifications 495-505mL. Samples of n=5 bottles taken every 30 minutes for the past 24 hours yield X-bar-bar = 500.2mL and R-bar = 2.8mL. Using A2 = 0.577 for n=5: UCL_Xbar = 500.2 + 0.577*2.8 = 501.8, LCL_Xbar = 498.6. Using D4 = 2.114 and D3 = 0 for n=5: UCL_R = 2.114*2.8 = 5.92, LCL_R = 0. Plot the 48 sample averages and ranges and identify two points beyond control limits. Investigate: a worn o-ring caused the morning drift, a cleaning cycle caused the range spike. Compute Cp = 10/(6*1.2) = 1.39 and Cpk = min[(505-500.2)/3.6, (500.2-495)/3.6] = min[1.33, 1.44] = 1.33. Process is capable but not Six Sigma; discuss options to reduce variation vs. recenter.
setup_requirements: Statistical software (Minitab, JMP) or Excel with control chart template
Sample datasets for variables and attributes data
Process capability calculation sheet
Case packet: 'Deming at Ford' and 'Six Sigma at GE'

review.value: Inspection catches defects after they are produced, but the defective units still cost money and some slip through; building quality into the process prevents defects from happening, which Deming argued is the only sustainable approach
Reacting to every fluctuation in a stable process (tampering) makes quality worse; SPC distinguishes normal variation from signals that demand investigation, preventing over-reaction

Cp measures whether the process could meet specs if perfectly centered; Cpk measures whether it actually does given its current center; a process with high Cp and low Cpk needs centering, not variation reduction

review.key: Cost of Quality Is a Lens on Priorities
review.value: Measuring COQ across the four categories usually reveals that external failure (warranty, returns) dominates total cost, justifying significant prevention investment; firms that do not measure COQ chronically underinvest in prevention

class_schedule.topic: Lean Operations and the Toyota Production System
learning_objectives: Identify the eight types of waste (muda) and explain how lean eliminates them
Describe the pillars of the Toyota Production System: just-in-time and jidoka
Apply lean tools: 5S, kaizen, kanban, value stream mapping, poka-yoke, and SMED
Contrast push and pull production systems and explain when each is appropriate
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 16
Ohno, Toyota Production System: Beyond Large-Scale Production (1988)
Womack, Jones & Roos, The Machine That Changed the World (1990)
Rother & Shook, Learning to See: Value Stream Mapping

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1950, Toyota was a small, financially fragile Japanese automaker producing fewer cars in a year than Ford made in a day. Its survival depended on becoming radically more productive with tiny volumes and limited capital. Taiichi Ohno and Eiji Toyoda developed what became the Toyota Production System, not as an abstract management theory but as an operational answer to an existential business problem. Over the next four decades, Toyota went from struggling domestic producer to the global benchmark for manufacturing productivity, quality, and profitability, and the Toyota Production System became the Lean movement that transformed industries from healthcare to software development. The core insight of lean is that most of the time and resources in a conventional operation are spent on activities that add no value from the customer's perspective: moving, waiting, inspecting, storing, reworking. Ohno classified seven wastes (muda), later extended to eight, and built a coherent system of tools and practices to expose and eliminate each one. Lean is not a collection of techniques but a philosophy: respect for people, relentless pursuit of flow, and obsessive focus on the customer. When implemented well, it delivers dramatic improvements in quality, cost, and delivery simultaneously. When implemented as a superficial toolkit without cultural commitment, it fails. This session develops both the conceptual framework and the practical tools.
key_concepts: The eight wastes (TIMWOODS): Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Overprocessing, Defects, Skills (underutilized human potential); identifying waste is the first step in lean improvement
Just-in-Time (JIT): produce only what is needed, when it is needed, in the amount needed; eliminates inventory waste but exposes all other problems that inventory was hiding

Jidoka (automation with a human touch): equipment stops automatically when a problem is detected, empowering workers to fix the root cause rather than passing defects downstream; embodied in the andon cord

Kanban: a physical or electronic signal that authorizes production or replenishment; enables pull production where downstream demand pulls units from upstream rather than upstream pushing inventory downstream
Push vs. pull: push systems produce based on forecasts and schedules, accumulating inventory if demand is lower; pull systems produce only in response to demand signals, preventing overproduction
5S: Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain; foundation for workplace organization and visual management; eliminates motion and searching waste and makes abnormalities visible
Kaizen: continuous incremental improvement driven by frontline workers; contrasts with big-bang reengineering; thousands of small improvements compound over years

Value stream mapping: diagramming every step from raw material to customer, with value-added vs. non-value-added time, to identify improvement opportunities; typically reveals 95%+ of lead time is waiting

Poka-yoke (mistake-proofing): designing processes and tools so that errors are impossible or immediately visible; examples include connectors that only fit one way, machines that will not start if a component is missing

SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die): reducing changeover time from hours to minutes through separating internal from external setup and standardizing procedures; enables small batch production essential to pull systems
Takt time: available production time divided by customer demand rate; sets the pace at which each unit must be produced to match demand without overproduction
lecture_notes.demonstration: Value stream map for a sandwich shop. Customer enters, orders at counter (30s), cashier rings up (60s), order goes to sandwich maker (wait 4 minutes during peak), sandwich is made (90s), handed back across counter (wait 30s), paid and completed. Total lead time = 7 minutes 30s. Value-added time (from customer perspective) = ordering (30s) + making (90s) + handing over (15s) = 2 minutes 15s. Process cycle efficiency = 135/450 = 30%. Identify waste: waiting, transportation between counter and maker, motion of maker reaching for ingredients. Apply lean tools: 5S the ingredient station (reduce motion), poka-yoke the ordering interface (prevent wrong orders), SMED the shift changes (reduce closed register time). Compare to Chipotle's open line which achieves higher PCE by eliminating the counter-to-maker handoff. Discuss how lean thinking applies equally to manufacturing and services.
setup_requirements: Value stream mapping template and symbols reference
Case packet: 'The Toyota Production System at Georgetown' and 'Lean at Virginia Mason Medical Center'
Video: Toyota Production System documentary
5S audit checklist

review.value: Lowering water level (inventory) exposes rocks (problems like quality defects, unreliable suppliers, long changeovers); reducing inventory without solving the underlying problems creates chaos, which is why lean implementation must be sequenced carefully

review.key: Pull Beats Push for Low Variety
review.value: When product variety is manageable and demand can be levelled, pull systems produce less inventory, shorter lead times, and better quality than forecast-driven push; very high variety or volatile demand may still require forecast-based planning

Lean tools without lean culture produce short-term gains that evaporate; sustainable lean requires training, trust, and empowerment so that frontline workers own continuous improvement rather than consultants doing it to them

A step that the customer would not pay for if given the choice is waste, no matter how efficiently it is performed; lean's discipline is to start from the customer and work backward, not from the current process and tinker with it

learning_objectives: Integrate forecasting, capacity, process, and quality tools into a coherent operations analysis
Diagnose operational performance problems using data and identify root causes
Communicate operational recommendations with quantitative support
Prepare for midterm examination covering Weeks 1-7
readings: Review: Heizer, Render & Munson, Chapters 1-9 and S6-S7
Case: 'National Cranberry Cooperative' (HBS 9-688-122)
Case: 'Shouldice Hospital Limited' (HBS 9-683-068)

lecture_notes.introduction: Midterm review sessions give you the chance to take a step back from individual tools and see how they connect. Real operational problems do not arrive labeled as forecasting problems or capacity problems; they arrive as complaints (customers are unhappy), symptoms (costs are rising), or events (a shipment was missed). The operations manager's job is to diagnose the real problem, decide which tools apply, and implement a solution. The National Cranberry Cooperative case is the canonical teaching case for process analysis: a cooperative receiving plant cannot process the cranberries fast enough during peak harvest, trucks wait for hours, and management must decide whether to add destoners, bins, or night shifts. The analysis requires applying cycle time, bottleneck identification, capacity, and Little's Law all at once. The Shouldice Hospital case examines a specialized hernia surgery hospital whose focused process design delivers exceptional clinical quality, short recovery times, and high patient satisfaction; it is a reminder that operations strategy, process choice, and service quality are deeply linked. Use today's session to pressure-test your understanding by attempting a complete analysis of one or both cases, and bring questions about the tools you find hardest.
key_concepts: Problem diagnosis: distinguish symptoms from causes; a long queue might be caused by insufficient capacity, unbalanced flow, poor scheduling, or variability
Data-driven recommendations: quantify the current state (throughput, capacity, utilization) and the future state under each alternative before recommending action

Integrated analysis: forecasting sets demand, capacity planning sets resources, process analysis governs flow, and quality sets acceptance criteria; changes to one domain have consequences in the others
National Cranberry: peak truck arrivals exceed destoner capacity, causing truck queues and overtime; solutions include adding destoners, staging bins, or leveling truck arrivals via pricing
Shouldice Hospital: focused factory for hernia surgery, standardized process, early ambulation, low infection rate, patient satisfaction; teaches the power of focus and operational specialization
Root cause analysis: 5 Whys technique traces symptoms backward through successive causes until reaching something actionable; often reveals systemic rather than local issues
Pareto principle: 80% of the problem usually comes from 20% of the causes; prioritize investigation and improvement accordingly
Communication of recommendations: lead with the decision and expected impact, then support with analysis; executives want clarity and confidence, not a walkthrough of every spreadsheet
lecture_notes.demonstration: Group work on National Cranberry. Given the peak day: 18,000 barrels received, 70% wet harvest requiring drying (wet drying rate 600 bbl/hr across 3 driers = 1800 bbl/hr combined), 30% dry skipping drying step; destoners process 3000 bbl/hr combined; bins hold 3200 bbl dry and 3200 bbl wet separately. Compute the bottleneck: wet flow = 18000*0.7 = 12600 bbl; wet drying capacity = 1800 bbl/hr; drying time alone = 7 hours. Given work day of 12 hours, are driers the bottleneck? Check destoner capacity for dry = 5400 bbl in 12 hours vs 12600 wet coming after drying means destoners process 12600 wet + 5400 dry = 18000 in 12 hours exactly if perfectly scheduled. Actual bottleneck is drying; solution is more driers or converting a destoner to a drier. Walk through the arithmetic step by step. Discuss why simply adding night shifts does not solve the peak-day problem.
setup_requirements: Case packets for National Cranberry and Shouldice Hospital
Midterm review sheet with formula summary
Spreadsheet for case calculations
Flip charts for group work

review.value: In any process analysis, the bottleneck determines throughput, so it is always the first thing to identify; improvements elsewhere are wasted effort and may even make things worse

Capacity decisions should consider peak load, not average; National Cranberry's capacity is adequate on an average day but fails on the 3-4 peak days per harvest, and that is the capacity that matters

Shouldice's clinical outcomes are superior precisely because the hospital does one thing; general hospitals cannot replicate them because generality prevents the deep process optimization focus allows
Midterm credit comes from correct reasoning and correct arithmetic; a right answer from wrong logic earns partial credit at best, and a wrong answer from right logic earns significant partial credit

class_schedule.topic: Inventory Management: EOQ, Safety Stock, and Reorder Points
learning_objectives: Classify inventory by ABC analysis and apply appropriate control policies to each class
Compute the economic order quantity (EOQ) and total cost under the assumptions of constant demand
Determine reorder point and safety stock given lead time and demand variability for a target service level
Analyze quantity discount opportunities and compute the optimal order quantity with discount pricing
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 12
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 20
Silver, Pyke & Thomas, Inventory and Production Management in Supply Chains (4th ed.), Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Inventory ties up capital, consumes storage space, obscures quality problems, and becomes obsolete, yet running without it means stockouts and lost customers. Managing the trade-off between too much and too little inventory is one of the oldest and most economically significant problems in operations. For Walmart, whose inventory turnover drives its competitive advantage, a 5% reduction in average inventory released billions of dollars for reinvestment. For Amazon, whose fulfillment network holds over $30 billion in inventory at any moment, inventory optimization directly affects cash flow, gross margin, and customer experience. For a small manufacturer, an EOQ calculation applied to raw materials can reduce inventory carrying cost by 20-30% without affecting production. The classical inventory models introduced in this session (EOQ, reorder point, safety stock) are approximations of a complex reality, but they clarify the underlying economics and provide defensible starting points. More sophisticated systems (such as s,S policies, multi-echelon inventory, and stochastic programming) build on these foundations. The point is not to believe EOQ's assumptions literally but to understand the logic it encodes: ordering costs and carrying costs trade off, and the optimal order size balances them.
key_concepts: Functions of inventory: decouple operations from demand, hedge against uncertainty, exploit quantity discounts, protect against supply disruption, and smooth seasonal demand

Inventory costs: purchasing (unit cost), ordering (fixed cost per order), carrying (storage, insurance, obsolescence, opportunity cost of capital; typically 20-30% of unit value per year), stockout (lost sales, backorders)
ABC analysis: classify items by annual dollar usage; A items (top 15% by count, 70-80% of value) get tight control, B items moderate control, C items loose control
EOQ assumptions: constant known demand, constant lead time, no quantity discounts, no stockouts; EOQ = sqrt(2DS/H) where D = annual demand, S = setup/order cost, H = annual holding cost per unit
EOQ total cost = (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H; at the optimum Q* = EOQ, the ordering cost and carrying cost are equal
EOQ is robust: because total cost is flat near the optimum, orders within plus/minus 30% of EOQ have nearly identical total cost; do not over-precisely compute EOQ
Reorder point (constant demand): ROP = d times L where d = daily demand and L = lead time in days; places the order so that the last unit arrives just as inventory reaches zero

Safety stock: protects against variability in demand and lead time; SS = z times sigma_DL where z is the service level standard normal value and sigma_DL is the standard deviation of demand during lead time

Service level: probability of not stocking out during a lead time cycle; common targets are 95% (z=1.65), 98% (z=2.05), 99% (z=2.33); higher service levels require disproportionately more safety stock
Reorder point with variability: ROP = d times L + SS; the first term covers expected demand, the second covers variability

Quantity discount model: total cost = purchasing + ordering + carrying; compute EOQ for each price level, check if that Q is feasible at that price, otherwise use the minimum feasible Q at that price, then pick the price level with lowest total cost
lecture_notes.demonstration: A hardware distributor sells 5000 units per year of a specialty valve. Unit cost is $50, ordering cost is $40 per order, annual carrying cost is 25% of unit value = $12.50. EOQ = sqrt(2*5000*40/12.50) = sqrt(32000) = 179 units. Number of orders per year = 5000/179 = 27.9. Annual ordering cost = 27.9*40 = $1,117. Annual carrying cost = (179/2)*12.50 = $1,119. Nearly equal, confirming optimum. Lead time = 5 days, daily demand = 5000/250 working days = 20/day. Reorder point without safety stock = 20*5 = 100 units. Now add variability: sigma_d = 4 units/day (historical standard deviation). Assume lead time constant. sigma_DL = sigma_d times sqrt(L) = 4 times sqrt(5) = 8.94. For 95% service level, z=1.65. Safety stock = 1.65 times 8.94 = 15 units. ROP with safety = 100 + 15 = 115 units. Now suppose supplier offers: 0-199 at $50, 200-499 at $48, 500+ at $46. Check each: at 500, annual cost = 5000*46 + (5000/500)*40 + (500/2)*(0.25*46) = 230000 + 400 + 2875 = $233,275. Compare to EOQ at $50: 250000 + 1117 + 1119 = $252,236. Buying 500 saves ~$19,000/year; accept the discount.
setup_requirements: Inventory calculation spreadsheet
Z-table for service level computations
Historical demand data for ABC analysis exercise
Case packet: 'Inventory Management at Walmart'

review.value: Trying to manage all inventory items with equal rigor wastes effort on items that do not matter; ABC classification focuses control on the 15% of items that drive 80% of inventory value
EOQ's assumptions are rarely strictly true, but the formula's logic is sound: ordering cost and carrying cost must balance, and the answer is robust to small deviations, making it a useful default

Moving from 95% to 99% service level can double safety stock; the last 1% of service is often the most expensive, and whether it is worth it depends on the cost of a stockout relative to the cost of holding the safety stock
Discounts change the optimum; intuition about 'buying in bulk' can be right or wrong depending on the carrying cost rate and the discount size, so always compare total cost across price levels

class_schedule.topic: Supply Chain Management and the Bullwhip Effect
learning_objectives: Describe the structure of a supply chain and the strategic choices that shape it
Explain the bullwhip effect, its causes, and its consequences for upstream suppliers
Analyze make-or-buy and outsourcing decisions using strategic and economic criteria
Evaluate sourcing strategies: single sourcing, multi-sourcing, and global sourcing, with their risks and benefits
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 11
Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (6th ed.), Ch. 1-4
Lee, Padmanabhan & Whang, 'The Bullwhip Effect in Supply Chains,' Sloan Management Review (1997)
Fisher, 'What Is the Right Supply Chain for Your Product?' Harvard Business Review (1997)

lecture_notes.introduction: No firm is an island; every product depends on a chain of suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, and retailers whose collective performance determines whether customers get what they want. Apple designs the iPhone but does not manufacture it; Foxconn and other contract manufacturers assemble the device using components from hundreds of suppliers across dozens of countries. Walmart's competitive edge comes less from its stores than from its supply chain: cross-docking, direct vendor relationships, and a proprietary information system that has become the industry standard. Zara's two-week design-to-shelf cycle is an operations feat that depends entirely on vertical integration of design, manufacturing in Spain and Morocco, and air freight to stores. When supply chains work well, they deliver products efficiently and invisibly. When they fail, the consequences are severe: the 2011 Thailand floods disrupted hard drive production worldwide, increasing prices 20-30% for months; the 2021 Suez Canal blockage stranded $10 billion of goods per day; the semiconductor shortage of 2020-2023 forced auto manufacturers to idle plants and cost the industry over $200 billion. Supply chain management requires understanding the structural choices that shape the chain, the behavioral dynamics that amplify disturbances, and the strategic trade-offs between efficiency and responsiveness.
key_concepts: Supply chain structure: suppliers (tier 1, 2, 3), manufacturer, distributors, retailers, customers; information, product, and money flow through all links

Fisher's framework: functional products (stable demand) need efficient supply chains (cost, high utilization, long lead times OK); innovative products (volatile demand) need responsive supply chains (flexibility, short lead times, higher costs)

The bullwhip effect: small variations in customer demand get amplified upstream, causing suppliers to experience massive demand swings; documented across industries by Procter & Gamble, HP, and others

Causes of bullwhip: demand signal processing (treating each order as new information), order batching (economies of scale in ordering), price fluctuations (forward buying during promotions), and rationing gaming (inflating orders when supply is tight)

Remedies for bullwhip: share point-of-sale data upstream (VMI, CPFR), reduce order cycle times and batch sizes, stabilize pricing (everyday low pricing), allocate scarce supply by historical sales not orders

Make vs. buy (vertical integration): make when the activity is core competency, critical to differentiation, or where suppliers lack capability; buy when scale economies, specialized suppliers, or flexibility matter more

Outsourcing strategy: identify activities, assess strategic importance and firm capability, outsource non-core but protect core; classic trap is outsourcing an activity that looks non-core but embeds strategic capability
Single sourcing: lower coordination cost, deeper partnership, volume leverage; but concentrates risk; Japanese auto firms use single sourcing with careful supplier development
Multi-sourcing: supply security, competitive pressure on suppliers, flexibility; but higher coordination cost and weaker partnership; U.S. auto firms historically used multi-sourcing
Global sourcing: lower unit cost but longer lead times, quality risk, currency exposure, political risk, and carbon footprint; reshoring movements since 2020 have reassessed global sourcing decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bullwhip simulation based on the Beer Distribution Game. In four tiers (retailer, wholesaler, distributor, brewery), start with stable demand of 10 cases per week. Week 5, consumer demand jumps from 10 to 15 cases. Each tier sees its incoming orders with a delay, panics, and overorders to rebuild inventory. Within weeks, the brewery sees orders of 40+ cases while actual consumer demand remains 15. The brewery increases production; when inventory finally catches up, the tiers all cut orders to near zero, causing the brewery to idle. Graph the order amplification factor by tier. Discuss how information sharing (giving the brewery direct visibility into retailer POS) would eliminate most of the effect. Compare to real cases: HP printer supply chain bullwhip, semiconductor industry cycles, Walmart's inventory management success.
setup_requirements: Beer Game simulator (online or board version)
Case packet: 'Barilla SpA' (HBS 9-694-046) and 'Zara: Fast Fashion'
Supply chain network diagram software
Spreadsheet for bullwhip calculations

review.value: Fisher's framework predicts which products need efficient vs. responsive supply chains; mismatches (e.g., fast fashion on an efficient supply chain, or commodity staples on a responsive one) are a main source of supply chain failure

review.key: Bullwhip Is Rational Individually, Irrational Collectively
review.value: Each actor in the chain is behaving rationally given their information, yet the collective result is whipsaw production, inventory costs, and service failures; the remedy is changing the information environment, not blaming individual actors
Walmart, Zara, and Amazon have proven that real-time information sharing across the chain reduces inventory while improving service; technology and trust are both required

review.key: Risk Is the Other Half of Cost
review.value: Sourcing decisions optimized purely on cost are fragile; the COVID-era disruptions reminded many firms that resilience has to be designed in, not bought after disaster strikes

learning_objectives: Distinguish between independent and dependent demand and explain why they require different planning approaches
Construct a bill of materials (BOM) and use it to explode a master production schedule into component requirements
Compute net requirements, planned order releases, and planned order receipts through time-phased MRP logic
Describe how ERP systems integrate MRP with financials, HR, and other enterprise functions
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 14
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 21
Vollmann, Berry, Whybark & Jacobs, Manufacturing Planning and Control for Supply Chain Management

lecture_notes.introduction: EOQ and reorder point models work well for independent-demand items like finished goods, where demand is driven by customers and can be treated as approximately independent period to period. They fail disastrously for dependent-demand items like the 10,000 components in an aircraft, the 5,000 parts in a car, or the 50 ingredients in a meal kit. For dependent demand, requirements are not independent: they are derived from the schedule of end items through the bill of materials. A single car on the schedule creates demand for exactly four tires, one engine, and so on; ordering tires based on their statistical independence rather than the car schedule leads to chronic shortages and surpluses. Joseph Orlicky and others at IBM in the 1960s recognized this and built Material Requirements Planning (MRP), which explodes the master production schedule through the BOM to compute net requirements for each component, period by period. MRP transformed manufacturing planning and became the foundation of modern enterprise resource planning (ERP). SAP, Oracle, and Microsoft Dynamics run the operations of most of the world's large manufacturers; understanding the MRP logic at their core is essential whether you will implement these systems, use them, or evaluate their output. MRP is mechanical but the assumptions and master data quality determine whether it produces useful or useless plans.
key_concepts: Independent demand: demand that is not derived from the demand for other products; forecasted from customer demand (e.g., finished cars, books in a retail store)
Dependent demand: demand that follows from the demand for a higher-level item through a bill of materials; computed not forecasted (e.g., tires for cars, pages for books)
Bill of materials (BOM): the list of components and subassemblies required to make one unit of a finished product, with quantities; often multi-level with subassemblies that have their own BOMs
Master production schedule (MPS): the schedule of finished items to produce, by quantity and period; the input to MRP explosion
Gross requirements: total demand for an item in each period, computed from parent item schedules via the BOM
Scheduled receipts: existing open orders expected to arrive in specific periods; count against future requirements
On-hand inventory: current stock; offsets near-term requirements
Net requirements = gross requirements - scheduled receipts - on-hand; only positive values generate planned orders
Planned order receipts: when and how much the MRP system plans to have arrive to cover net requirements
Planned order releases: when orders must be released to production or suppliers, given lead time; = planned order receipts offset by lead time
Lot sizing rules: lot-for-lot (order exactly the net requirement, no inventory), fixed order quantity, EOQ, or periodic order quantity; choice affects carrying and setup costs

ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning): integration of MRP with financial accounting, HR, procurement, CRM, and reporting into a single enterprise system; major vendors are SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics; implementations are multi-year and expensive
Master data quality: MRP output is only as good as the BOM, routings, lead times, and inventory accuracy; poor master data is the single biggest reason MRP implementations fail
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work a full MRP explosion for a bicycle. BOM: Bicycle = 1 frame, 2 wheels, 1 handlebar; Wheel = 1 rim, 1 tire, 1 hub. MPS calls for 100 bicycles in week 6 and 150 in week 8. Starting inventory: frames 20, wheels 40, handlebars 50, rims 60, tires 50, hubs 40. Lead times: bicycle assembly 1 week, frame 2 weeks, wheel assembly 1 week, handlebar 1 week, rim 2 weeks, tire 1 week, hub 2 weeks. Compute gross requirements level by level: 100 bikes in wk 6 means 100 frames, 200 wheels, 100 handlebars in wk 5; 150 bikes in wk 8 means 150 frames, 300 wheels, 150 handlebars in wk 7. Wheel demand drives rims, tires, hubs. Net out by subtracting on-hand and scheduled receipts. Offset by lead time to get planned order releases. Show the time-phased grid for each item. Discuss what happens if the bicycle lead time is actually 2 weeks and the supplier misses delivery: cascading shortages appear throughout the BOM.
setup_requirements: MRP spreadsheet template with BOM explosion
Case packet: 'Cisco Systems ERP Implementation' (HBS 9-699-022)
Sample BOMs for several products
ERP demo environment (SAP learning hub or ERPNext)

review.value: The single most important distinction in production planning; applying statistical inventory models to dependent items guarantees failure, and computing dependent demand via MRP exposes the actual requirements pattern

review.key: MRP Is Mechanical, Plans Are Not
review.value: MRP crunches the numbers given the inputs; whether the resulting plan is feasible depends on capacity (which basic MRP ignores), supplier reliability, and data quality; CRP and APS tools add capacity checks

Incorrect BOMs, outdated routings, and inaccurate inventory balances degrade MRP output silently; firms with 98%+ inventory accuracy and disciplined engineering change management get the full benefit of MRP

ERP systems touch every function of the firm; a failed ERP implementation (Hershey, Nike, Waste Management) can paralyze the business for quarters, which is why implementation governance and change management matter as much as software selection

learning_objectives: Apply single-machine sequencing rules (FCFS, SPT, EDD) and evaluate performance measures
Schedule jobs across multiple machines using Johnson's rule for two-machine flow shop
Construct a PERT/CPM network, compute earliest and latest start/finish times, and identify the critical path
Apply probabilistic project analysis using PERT three-time estimates
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 3 and Ch. 15
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 4
Pinedo, Scheduling: Theory, Algorithms, and Systems (5th ed.), Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Scheduling is the tactical layer between planning and execution: given the plan, in what order and at what time do we actually do the work? A hospital operating room schedule determines whether the surgical suite is utilized efficiently and whether emergency cases can be accommodated. A software project schedule determines whether a release ships on time. A manufacturer's shop floor schedule determines daily throughput, on-time delivery, and work-in-process inventory. Bad scheduling wastes capacity, misses commitments, and creates chaos even when every other part of operations is well-designed. Scheduling comes in two major flavors: job-shop and project scheduling. In job-shop scheduling, we have many jobs competing for limited machines, and the question is in what sequence to process them to minimize makespan, tardiness, or work-in-process. Simple rules like shortest processing time and earliest due date are surprisingly effective despite their simplicity. In project scheduling, a single project has many interdependent tasks, and the question is which tasks lie on the critical path (and thus delay the project if delayed) and which have slack. The Apollo program, Boeing 787 development, and every major construction project rely on PERT/CPM techniques. Without them, you cannot know which tasks to expedite when time slips.
key_concepts: Sequencing rules for single machine: FCFS (first come first served), SPT (shortest processing time), EDD (earliest due date), LPT (longest processing time), CR (critical ratio = time remaining / work remaining)
SPT minimizes average flow time, average number of jobs in system, and average lateness; it is provably optimal for these measures on a single machine
EDD minimizes maximum lateness (maximum tardiness of any job); useful when missed deadlines have non-linear penalties
FCFS is fair and easy to explain but performs poorly on any efficiency measure; still widely used in queues where fairness matters (customer service)
Johnson's rule for two-machine flow shop: sort jobs so that those with smallest time on machine 1 go first and those with smallest time on machine 2 go last; minimizes makespan
Project: a set of interdependent activities with a defined start, end, budget, and resources; scheduling requires both precedence and duration data
PERT/CPM network: activities on nodes (AON) or activities on arcs (AOA); precedence arrows show what must finish before what can start
Earliest start (ES) and earliest finish (EF) computed by forward pass; latest start (LS) and latest finish (LF) computed by backward pass; slack = LS - ES
Critical path: the longest path through the network; activities on the critical path have zero slack and directly determine project duration
PERT three-time estimates: optimistic (a), most likely (m), pessimistic (b); expected duration = (a + 4m + b)/6, variance = ((b-a)/6)^2
Project duration is approximately normally distributed; use the sum of variances on the critical path to compute the probability of finishing by a target date
Crashing: expedite activities on the critical path at extra cost to shorten project; compare cost per day saved to benefit of finishing earlier; beware that crashing may create new critical paths
lecture_notes.demonstration: Schedule five jobs on a single machine and compare FCFS vs. SPT vs. EDD. Jobs (time, due): A (6, 8), B (2, 6), C (8, 18), D (3, 15), E (9, 23). FCFS order ABCDE: flow times 6, 8, 16, 19, 28; avg flow = 15.4; lateness 0, 2, 0, 4, 5, max = 5. SPT order BDACE: 2, 5, 11, 19, 28; avg flow = 13; lateness 0, 0, 3, 1, 5, max = 5. EDD order BADCE: 2, 8, 11, 19, 28; avg flow = 13.6; lateness 0, 0, 0, 4, 5, max = 5. SPT wins on average flow as predicted. Now a PERT network for a product launch. Activities A-H with durations and precedences; walk through forward and backward pass; identify critical path; compute probability of finishing within 20 weeks using PERT variance calculations.
setup_requirements: Project scheduling software (MS Project, GanttPro) or PERT/CPM spreadsheet
Sample project dataset with 15+ activities
Sequencing exercise packet
Gantt chart drawing software

review.key: SPT Is the Default for Efficiency
review.value: Whenever minimizing average completion time or work in process matters, SPT is hard to beat; it is why fast-food restaurants process drive-thru orders from fastest to slowest when the kitchen allows
Accelerating a non-critical activity does not shorten the project; only critical path activities do, and managers should focus attention and resources there
Activities with zero slack cannot be delayed; activities with large slack can be deferred if resources are needed elsewhere; monitoring slack is how project managers triage risk
Point estimates lie; three-point PERT estimates allow probabilistic statements about finish dates, which executives need for commitments and risk management

class_schedule.topic: Facility Location, Global Operations, and Sustainability
learning_objectives: Apply factor rating and center-of-gravity methods to facility location decisions
Evaluate trade-offs in global operations including labor cost, logistics, and political risk
Explain the triple bottom line and sustainable operations principles
Assess environmental impact of operations decisions using life-cycle analysis concepts
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 8 and Ch. 5
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 15
Porter & van der Linde, 'Green and Competitive,' Harvard Business Review (1995)
Elkington, Cannibals with Forks: The Triple Bottom Line (1997)

lecture_notes.introduction: Where to locate a facility is one of the longest-lasting operations decisions a firm can make. BMW's 1994 decision to build its first U.S. plant in Spartanburg, South Carolina still shapes its supply chain, workforce, and cost structure three decades later. Foxconn's announced 2017 Wisconsin plant became a cautionary tale about the gap between promises and execution. Toyota's decision to locate North American operations in Kentucky and Texas, rather than closer to Detroit's supplier base, was deliberate: it placed production in states with lower labor costs and less unionization. Location decisions incorporate tangible factors (labor cost, logistics, taxes, land) and intangible factors (political stability, workforce quality, community attitudes, proximity to customers and suppliers). They also increasingly incorporate sustainability: climate risk, water availability, carbon footprint, and regulatory environment can dominate pure cost analyses over a plant's 20-40 year life. Sustainability is no longer peripheral to operations management. Unilever, Patagonia, and Interface have shown that sustainable operations can reduce cost, improve brand, and attract talent simultaneously. Firms that ignore environmental impact face rising regulatory pressure, reputational risk, and stranded assets. This session covers both the quantitative techniques for facility location and the broader framing of sustainability in operations decisions.
key_concepts: Factor rating method: list critical factors, assign weights summing to 1.0, score each location on each factor (e.g., 1-100), compute weighted score; handles qualitative factors that defy pure cost analysis
Center of gravity: for a distribution problem, the optimal location (x*, y*) minimizes weighted distance; computed as (sum of Qi*xi / sum Qi, sum Qi*yi / sum Qi) where Qi is volume to location i
Locational break-even analysis: compute total cost at each candidate location as a function of volume; different locations dominate at different production volumes
Global operations drivers: access to markets, access to lower-cost labor or materials, access to skills and technology, political incentives, hedging against currency or trade risk
Global operations risks: currency fluctuation, trade barriers, geopolitical instability, longer and more fragile supply chains, quality control over distance, reputational risk from labor practices
Reshoring trends: rising wages in China, shipping cost volatility, COVID supply shocks, and geopolitical tensions have led firms to reconsider offshoring decisions of the 1990s-2010s
Triple bottom line: people, planet, profit; sustainability performance measured across social, environmental, and economic dimensions, not just financial
Life cycle analysis: cradle-to-grave environmental impact of a product, from raw material extraction through manufacturing, distribution, use, and disposal; reveals unexpected hotspots
Sustainable operations practices: energy efficiency, renewable energy, water conservation, waste reduction and recycling, green chemistry, closed-loop supply chains, circular economy
Carbon footprint: total greenhouse gas emissions attributable to a product or operation; increasingly measured, reported, and regulated (CDP, SBTi, EU CSRD)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Factor rating for locating a semiconductor packaging plant among three candidates: Austin, Texas; Penang, Malaysia; Tainan, Taiwan. Factors and weights: labor cost (0.25), workforce skill (0.20), proximity to wafer fabs (0.20), political stability (0.15), water availability (0.10), tax incentives (0.10). Score each on 1-100 scale. Compute weighted score. Austin scores well on political stability and tax incentives but low on skill depth and proximity; Penang scores well on labor cost and skill; Tainan scores well on proximity (most wafer fabs are in Taiwan) but poorly on political stability (geopolitical risk). Discuss how the weights implicitly encode strategy, and how different weights would yield different choices. Extend to center of gravity: given four fab locations and their volumes, compute the optimal packaging plant location.
setup_requirements: Factor rating spreadsheet template
Coordinate and volume data for center of gravity exercise
Life cycle analysis software (openLCA or SimaPro) or case examples
Case packet: 'BMW Spartanburg' and 'Patagonia: Sustainable Operations'

review.value: A purely cost-driven location decision misses the qualitative factors (workforce, community, risk) that often dominate long-term outcomes; factor rating makes the weighting explicit and debatable
Total landed cost includes shipping, inventory in transit, duty, quality losses, and coordination overhead; low wages alone do not guarantee savings, as many reshored operations have discovered

Energy efficiency, waste reduction, and water management are both environmental goals and cost-reduction opportunities; Porter and van der Linde argued that environmental regulation can spur innovation and competitive advantage
Unlike most operations decisions, a facility location is sticky for decades; make it with a long horizon and include scenario planning for climate, policy, and technology shifts

class_schedule.topic: Service Operations and Capacity Management in Services
learning_objectives: Explain how services differ from goods and how these differences affect operations
Apply queuing analysis to service capacity decisions (M/M/1, M/M/c)
Design service processes with appropriate customer contact and line of visibility
Apply yield management principles to perishable capacity
readings: Heizer, Render & Munson, Operations Management (13th ed.), Ch. 7 and module D
Fitzsimmons, Fitzsimmons & Bordoloi, Service Management (8th ed.), Ch. 4-6
Chase, 'Where Does the Customer Fit in a Service Operation?' Harvard Business Review (1978)
Hopp & Lovejoy, Hospital Operations: Principles of High Efficiency Health Care

lecture_notes.introduction: Services are more than 70% of the U.S. economy and a growing share of global GDP, yet much of operations management's classical theory was developed with factories in mind. Services differ from goods in ways that matter for operations: they are intangible, produced and consumed simultaneously, perishable (an empty airline seat on a departed flight is lost forever), and variable because customers participate in production. These differences do not mean operations tools do not apply; they mean the tools must be adapted.
