Queuing theory is the mathematical foundation for service capacity decisions where arrivals are random and servers have variable processing times. Understanding why a 95% utilized server has dramatically longer wait times than an 80% utilized server prevents the common mistake of setting staffing as if service demand were perfectly predictable. Service design decisions about what the customer sees (line of visibility) and what happens backstage determine the perceived quality of the service experience. Yield management, pioneered by American Airlines after deregulation, turned perishable capacity into a revenue management problem: airlines, hotels, car rentals, and now rideshare all use algorithmic pricing to maximize revenue given inventory of perishable capacity. This session brings operations tools to bear on the modern service economy.
key_concepts: Service characteristics: intangibility, simultaneous production and consumption, heterogeneity (variability), perishability; each implies specific operational challenges

Customer contact and its operational implications: high-contact systems (restaurants, hair salons) have more variability and customization; low-contact systems (online banking) can be engineered for efficiency
Service blueprint: process map with line of visibility separating front-stage from back-stage activities; tool for service design and gap analysis

Queuing system elements: arrival process (usually Poisson for independent arrivals), service process (often exponential), number of servers, queue discipline (FCFS, priority), queue capacity (finite or infinite)
M/M/1 (single server, Poisson arrivals, exponential service): rho = lambda/mu utilization; L = rho/(1-rho) expected number in system; W = 1/(mu-lambda) expected time in system
M/M/c (c servers): more complex formula but same qualitative behavior; as utilization approaches 1, waiting time explodes
The cost of utilization: plotting wait time vs. utilization shows an exponential curve; adding capacity to drop from 95% to 85% utilization can halve wait times
Psychology of waiting (Maister): unoccupied time feels longer, uncertain waits feel longer, unfair waits feel longer, unexplained waits feel longer; operational design can manage perception

Yield management (revenue management): dynamic pricing and inventory allocation for perishable capacity with heterogeneous customers; originated in airlines (American Airlines SABRE), now used in hotels, rental cars, rideshare
Overbooking: systematic overselling of perishable capacity to offset no-shows, calibrated to expected no-show rate and the cost of bumping a customer vs. the cost of an empty seat
Service level agreements (SLAs): contractual performance commitments (uptime, response time, resolution time); must be designed to be achievable at the committed capacity and verifiable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Queuing analysis for a hospital emergency department. Arrivals: Poisson with mean 8 patients per hour. Service: exponential with mean 6 minutes per patient. With 1 doctor: mu = 10/hour, rho = 8/10 = 0.8. L = 0.8/0.2 = 4 patients in system on average; W = 1/(10-8) = 0.5 hours = 30 minutes average time in system. Utilization seems high but feasible. Now demand grows to 9.5/hour: rho = 0.95, L = 19, W = 2 hours - unacceptable. Add second doctor (M/M/2): waiting time drops back to minutes. Compute the marginal value of the second doctor (reduction in wait time per dollar of doctor salary) and compare to the consequences of the longer wait (hospital reputation, bad outcomes from delayed care, patient diversion). Extend with priority queuing: critical cases seen immediately regardless of arrival order, reflecting real ED triage.
setup_requirements: Queuing calculator or simulation tool (QTS or SimQuick)
Case packet: 'Yield Management at American Airlines' and 'Cleveland Clinic: Patient Flow'
Service blueprint templates
Historical arrival data from a service operation

review.value: Queues form when arrivals and service times are variable relative to capacity; reducing variability (smoothing arrivals, standardizing service times) is often cheaper than adding capacity
Running servers at 90%+ utilization guarantees long waits when variability is present; operational managers who target very high utilization are guaranteeing service failure
What customers see shapes their perception of service quality more than backstage reality; making waits visible, predictable, and fair is often more valuable than shortening them
Any capacity that cannot be stored (seats, rooms, time slots) must be sold at prices that reflect expected demand and remaining capacity; uniform pricing leaves revenue on the table

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Topics: AI, Automation, Resilience, and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Evaluate AI and automation opportunities across operations functions with attention to both benefits and risks
Design operations for resilience in the face of disruption
Synthesize course concepts into a framework for operational decision-making
Prepare for the final project and exam
readings: Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 25
McKinsey & Company, 'The State of AI in Operations' (2023)
Sheffi, The Resilient Enterprise (2007)
Harvard Business Review, 'Global Supply Chains in a Post-Pandemic World' (2020)

lecture_notes.introduction: The past five years have forced operations managers to confront two converging challenges: the rapid maturation of AI and automation technologies that promise to transform every operation, and a series of disruptions (COVID, semiconductor shortages, war in Ukraine, climate events) that revealed how fragile globally optimized supply chains can be. The firms that navigated these challenges best were neither the most cost-efficient nor the most technologically advanced; they were the ones that had built in resilience and adaptability before they needed it. Amazon's pandemic-era hiring and warehouse buildout, Maersk's rapid recovery from the 2017 NotPetya cyberattack, and Taiwan Semiconductor's continued output through pandemic lockdowns all illustrate the value of prepared flexibility. Meanwhile, AI has moved from pilot projects to production deployment in forecasting (Walmart, Amazon), predictive maintenance (GE, Siemens), quality inspection (BMW, Foxconn), and autonomous logistics (Waymo, Nuro). The question is no longer whether to adopt AI in operations but which applications justify the investment and how to deploy them without creating new failure modes. This final session synthesizes the course's toolkit and frames the judgment questions that will define the next decade of operations management. The goal is not to memorize more techniques but to develop the judgment to apply the right tool to the right problem and know when the model is failing you.
key_concepts: AI in forecasting: machine learning models (gradient boosting, neural networks) often outperform classical methods on high-dimensional data with many features; require disciplined feature engineering and data hygiene
Predictive maintenance: sensors, IoT, and ML models predict equipment failure before it happens, enabling planned interventions; requires investment in instrumentation and data infrastructure
Computer vision for quality inspection: CNNs detect surface defects at speeds and accuracies human inspectors cannot match; deployed at Tesla, Samsung, BMW; reduces escape rate and cost
Robotic process automation (RPA) and intelligent automation: automate rule-based work (invoice processing, order entry) freeing humans for judgment-intensive work

Resilience vs. efficiency trade-off: lean systems optimized for efficiency sacrifice buffers that protect against disruption; resilient systems keep some inventory, multiple suppliers, and redundant capacity
Risk management strategies: supply diversification, geographic redundancy, buffer inventory at critical points, supplier qualification and audits, business continuity planning, insurance
Digital twins: virtual replicas of physical assets or processes that enable simulation, optimization, and what-if analysis; used by Unilever, Rolls-Royce, and Siemens
Sustainability and ESG operations: carbon accounting, scope 1/2/3 emissions, science-based targets, circular economy, and regulatory compliance (EU CSRD, SEC climate disclosure)

Operational capabilities as strategic asset: firms like Toyota, Amazon, and TSMC compete on operational capabilities that competitors cannot quickly replicate because they emerge from decades of investment and culture

Decision framework synthesis: (1) understand strategy and competitive priorities; (2) diagnose the process; (3) quantify with the right tool; (4) consider variability, risk, and resilience; (5) sequence changes to manage implementation risk
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case synthesis: analyze how a hypothetical consumer electronics firm should respond to a major supplier disruption. Walk through all the tools from the course. Forecasting: what does demand look like for the next 6 months with and without the disruption? Inventory: should safety stocks be raised? Supply chain: should an alternate supplier be qualified? Capacity: can production be reallocated across the firm's plants? Quality: will the alternate supplier meet standards? Operations strategy: does the firm's competitive position require continued supply at any cost, or does it permit short-term shortage? Resilience: what lessons suggest structural changes to avoid future disruptions? Discuss the tension between resilience investments (carrying cost) and the value of protection against rare events.
setup_requirements: Final project instructions packet
Case packet: 'Supply Chain Disruption at Apple' and 'AI at Walmart'
Exam review sheet with all formulas
Course synthesis framework handout

review.value: Every tool in this course has assumptions; using EOQ when demand is lumpy, or queuing formulas when arrivals are not Poisson, produces misleading answers; the operations manager's job is knowing when to trust the model and when to override it

Resilience does not emerge from pure efficiency optimization; it requires conscious choices about buffers, redundancy, and flexibility that pay off only when disruptions occur; organizations that defer these investments pay for them in crisis

review.key: AI Does Not Replace Operations Judgment
review.value: AI can forecast, classify, and optimize, but it cannot decide strategy, manage stakeholders, or take responsibility for decisions; it extends the operations manager's toolkit, it does not substitute for the manager

Firms with superior operations can pursue strategic positions their competitors cannot match; in a world of rapid technology change and volatile environments, operational capability may be the most durable competitive advantage a firm can build

questions.question: A commercial bakery produces two product lines: artisan bread and packaged cookies. Last month it produced 4,000 loaves of bread (sold at $6 each) and 10,000 boxes of cookies (sold at $4 each). Labor hours: 220 for bread, 180 for cookies. Materials cost $0.80 per loaf and $0.60 per box. Labor cost is $22/hour. Overhead is $6,000/month allocated 50-50 between lines. (a) Compute labor productivity (units per hour) for each line. (b) Compute multifactor productivity (revenue per dollar of input) for each line. (c) Which line is more productive, and which should the firm expand given fixed labor hours? Justify.
questions.answer:
(a) Labor productivity:
- Bread: 4,000 / 220 = 18.18 loaves per labor hour
- Cookies: 10,000 / 180 = 55.56 boxes per labor hour

(b) Multifactor productivity (revenue per dollar of input):
- Bread inputs: labor 220*$22 = $4,840; materials 4,000*$0.80 = $3,200; overhead $3,000; total $11,040. Revenue = 4,000*$6 = $24,000. MFP = 24,000/11,040 = 2.17
- Cookies inputs: labor 180*$22 = $3,960; materials 10,000*$0.60 = $6,000; overhead $3,000; total $12,960. Revenue = 10,000*$4 = $40,000. MFP = 40,000/12,960 = 3.09

(c) Labor productivity metric favors cookies (55.6 vs 18.2 units/hr) but this is misleading because units are not comparable between products.

Multifactor productivity favors cookies ($3.09 of revenue per input dollar vs $2.17 for bread).

For expansion with fixed labor hours, compute contribution margin per labor hour:
- Bread: ($6 - $0.80) * 4,000 / 220 = $20,800 / 220 = $94.55 per labor hour
- Cookies: ($4 - $0.60) * 10,000 / 180 = $34,000 / 180 = $188.89 per labor hour

Cookies generate more than twice the contribution margin per labor hour. Given fixed labor constraint, expand cookies. However, before committing, consider market saturation, capacity constraints (oven time), and strategic fit.

rubric.key: Correct labor productivity calculations for both lines
Correct multifactor productivity with all inputs included
Correct contribution margin per labor hour computation
Reasoned recommendation addressing trade-offs beyond pure productivity

questions.question: A regional airline's monthly passenger counts for the past 12 months (Jan through Dec) were: 45, 42, 52, 68, 78, 95, 110, 108, 85, 64, 48, 55 (thousands). (a) Forecast January of the next year using a 3-month moving average. (b) Forecast using exponential smoothing with alpha=0.3 and an initial forecast of 50. (c) Describe why both methods are likely to produce poor forecasts and recommend a better approach.
questions.answer:
(a) 3-month moving average for next January = (Oct + Nov + Dec)/3 = (64 + 48 + 55)/3 = 55.67 thousand passengers.

(b) Exponential smoothing with alpha=0.3, F1 = 50:
F2 = 0.3*45 + 0.7*50 = 48.5
F3 = 0.3*42 + 0.7*48.5 = 46.55
F4 = 0.3*52 + 0.7*46.55 = 48.19
F5 = 0.3*68 + 0.7*48.19 = 54.13
F6 = 0.3*78 + 0.7*54.13 = 61.29
F7 = 0.3*95 + 0.7*61.29 = 71.40
F8 = 0.3*110 + 0.7*71.40 = 82.98
F9 = 0.3*108 + 0.7*82.98 = 90.49
F10 = 0.3*85 + 0.7*90.49 = 88.84
F11 = 0.3*64 + 0.7*88.84 = 81.39
F12 = 0.3*48 + 0.7*81.39 = 71.37
F13 (Jan next year) = 0.3*55 + 0.7*71.37 = 66.46

Forecast: ~66.5 thousand passengers for January.

(c) Both forecasts are problematic because the data is strongly seasonal: clear summer peak (July 110, Aug 108) and winter trough (Jan 45, Feb 42). Moving average uses only the most recent three months, producing a forecast near 56 that misses the actual January seasonal trough. Exponential smoothing lags the data and produces 66, even farther from last January's 45.

Better approach: decompose the time series into level, trend, and seasonality. Compute the annual average = 837/12 = 69.75. Compute each month's seasonal index = (actual / annual average). Last January's index = 45/69.75 = 0.645. Forecast next January = estimated level for next year * 0.645. With no strong trend, level estimate ~70, so forecast ~45. Or use Winter's exponential smoothing method which handles level, trend, and seasonality simultaneously. Any method that ignores seasonality in strongly seasonal data will produce large errors.

rubric.key: Correct moving average calculation
Correct exponential smoothing progression through all 12 months
Identification of seasonality as the key issue
Recommendation of a seasonal method (decomposition or Winter's)

questions.question: Southwest Airlines and Singapore Airlines both compete in commercial aviation but have very different operations strategies. Using the concepts of order-qualifiers, order-winners, and Hayes-Wheelwright stages: (a) Identify likely order-qualifiers and order-winners for each airline's target customer. (b) Explain how each airline's structural and infrastructural operations decisions align with these priorities. (c) Argue whether either airline is at Stage 4 (externally supportive) in the Hayes-Wheelwright framework.
questions.answer:
(a) Southwest Airlines - Low-cost domestic traveler:
- Order-qualifiers: safety record, on-time performance meeting industry norms, basic cleanliness
- Order-winners: low price, direct flights, friendly service, no hidden fees

Singapore Airlines - Premium international traveler:
- Order-qualifiers: competitive price for premium class, reliable schedules
- Order-winners: cabin quality and amenities, in-flight service, lounges, network to Asia-Pacific, brand prestige

(b) Southwest operations alignment:
- Structural: single aircraft type (Boeing 737) reduces pilot training, maintenance, spare parts inventory
- Structural: point-to-point network (not hub-and-spoke) reduces aircraft turnaround complexity
- Structural: secondary airports (lower landing fees, less congestion)
- Infrastructural: cross-trained ground crews for fast 25-minute turnarounds
- Infrastructural: open seating eliminates boarding delays
- Infrastructural: strong culture of efficiency and friendliness

Singapore Airlines operations alignment:
- Structural: wide-body fleet optimized for long-haul international routes
- Structural: hub in Singapore Changi (award-winning airport supporting premium experience)
- Structural: investment in lie-flat seats, premium cabins, lounges
- Infrastructural: extensive flight attendant training (7.5 months, among industry's longest)
- Infrastructural: meticulous service quality standards and in-flight procedures
- Infrastructural: brand-reinforcing uniforms, catering, entertainment selection

(c) Stage 4 analysis:
Southwest: Yes, Stage 4. Its operations model (secondary airports, single fleet, fast turns, point-to-point) is the source of its cost advantage and strategic position. Competitors have repeatedly tried to copy it (Delta Song, United Ted) and failed because the operational capabilities cannot be easily replicated without also replicating the culture and structural decisions.

Singapore Airlines: Yes, Stage 4. Its service quality is a sustainable competitive advantage rooted in deep operational capabilities (training, standards, culture) that competitors cannot quickly match. Competitors occasionally match cabin hardware but rarely the consistency of service delivery, because that requires decades of operational investment.

rubric.key: Correct identification of qualifiers vs. winners for each carrier
Specific structural and infrastructural decisions linked to priorities
Valid Stage 4 argument with the specific criteria (distinctive, aligned, hard to imitate)

questions.question: A coffee shop has three stations: order-taking (25 sec/customer), drink preparation (40 sec/customer), and payment/handoff (20 sec/customer). Each station has one employee. Customers arrive at 70 per hour during the morning rush. (a) Identify the bottleneck and compute maximum throughput. (b) Compute cycle time and utilization of each station. (c) Apply Little's Law to find average WIP (customers in the system) if average flow time is 3 minutes. (d) Recommend specific improvements to increase throughput, with quantitative estimates.
questions.answer:
(a) Bottleneck and maximum throughput:
- Order: 25 sec -> 3600/25 = 144 customers/hour capacity
- Prep: 40 sec -> 3600/40 = 90 customers/hour capacity
- Payment: 20 sec -> 3600/20 = 180 customers/hour capacity

Bottleneck = drink preparation (40 sec, lowest capacity). Maximum throughput = 90 customers/hour.

At demand of 70/hour, system is not yet fully saturated but prep is running at high utilization.

(b) Cycle time (time between consecutive customers leaving) = bottleneck time = 40 sec.

Utilization at demand of 70/hour:
- Order: 70*25/3600 = 0.486 = 48.6%
- Prep: 70*40/3600 = 0.778 = 77.8%
- Payment: 70*20/3600 = 0.389 = 38.9%

Prep is running at 77.8% utilization, which will create queues as variability enters.

(c) Little's Law: WIP = throughput * flow time
Throughput = 70 customers/hour = 70/60 = 1.167 per minute
Flow time = 3 minutes
WIP = 1.167 * 3 = 3.5 customers in the system on average.

(d) Improvements:
1. Add a second prep station or employee: doubles prep capacity to 180/hour, shifts bottleneck to order-taking (144/hour). New max throughput = 144/hour, a 60% increase.
2. Reduce prep time via process improvement (pre-measured ingredients, better layout, mistake-proofing): if prep drops to 30 sec, capacity = 120/hour.
3. Mobile ordering eliminates the in-store order-taking step for those customers, reducing load on order and potentially making payment the bottleneck with capacity 180/hour - a more than 2x improvement.

The best short-term action is usually to add prep capacity (the current bottleneck) because it directly increases throughput.

rubric.key: Correct bottleneck identification and throughput calculation
Correct utilization for all three stations
Correct Little's Law application with units
Specific quantified improvement recommendations focused on the bottleneck

questions.question: An automotive parts supplier has been running an X-bar and R chart for a critical diameter measurement. Sample size n=4, sampled every hour. Over 30 samples, X-bar-bar = 12.005 mm, R-bar = 0.018 mm. Specifications are 12.000 +/- 0.020 mm. Use A2 = 0.729, D3 = 0, D4 = 2.282 for n=4. (a) Compute control limits for X-bar and R charts. (b) Estimate process standard deviation using R-bar / d2 where d2 = 2.059. (c) Compute Cp and Cpk. (d) Is the process capable? Six Sigma quality? What action would you recommend?
questions.answer:
(a) Control limits:

X-bar chart:
UCL = 12.005 + 0.729 * 0.018 = 12.0181 mm
LCL = 12.005 - 0.01312 = 11.9919 mm
Center line = 12.005 mm

R chart:
UCL = 2.282 * 0.018 = 0.0411 mm
LCL = 0
Center line = 0.018 mm

(b) Process standard deviation:
sigma = R-bar / d2 = 0.018 / 2.059 = 0.00874 mm

(c) Process capability:
USL = 12.020, LSL = 11.980, tolerance width = 0.040

Cp = 0.040 / (6 * 0.00874) = 0.040 / 0.0524 = 0.763

Cpk = min[(12.020 - 12.005)/0.02622, (12.005 - 11.980)/0.02622]
    = min[0.572, 0.953]
    = 0.572

(d) Assessment and action:

The process is NOT capable. Cp = 0.763 < 1.0 means the natural process variation (6 sigma = 0.0524 mm) is wider than the specification width (0.040 mm). Even a perfectly centered process would produce out-of-spec parts.

Cpk = 0.572 < 1.0 confirms actual capability is worse than potential; the process is both too variable AND off-center.

Six Sigma quality requires Cpk >= 2.0; this process at 0.572 is far from Six Sigma.

Recommended actions:
1. Recenter the process: shift the mean from 12.005 toward 12.000 (the spec target). Recentering alone would improve Cpk to about 0.763 (matching Cp), still not capable.
2. Reduce variation: to achieve Cp = 1.33 (commonly required minimum), need sigma = 0.040 / (6 * 1.33) = 0.00501 mm, about 43% reduction from current 0.00874. Requires root cause investigation: tool wear, material variation, operator effects, ambient conditions.
3. In the meantime, increase inspection or sort parts to prevent out-of-spec product from reaching the customer.

rubric.key: Correct X-bar and R chart control limits
Correct sigma estimate and Cp calculation
Correct Cpk calculation recognizing centering effect
Correct interpretation and prioritized action recommendations

questions.question: A hospital is deciding whether to expand its emergency department. Current capacity handles 40,000 visits per year with $8M annual fixed cost and $250 variable cost per visit. A larger facility would handle 80,000 visits per year with $14M annual fixed cost and $180 variable cost per visit. Average revenue per visit is $550. (a) Compute break-even volume for each option. (b) Compute the indifference volume at which the two options have equal total cost. (c) Forecast says next year demand will be 50,000 (p=0.4), 65,000 (p=0.35), or 85,000 visits (p=0.25). Build a decision tree and compute EMV. (d) Beyond EMV, what qualitative factors should influence this decision?
questions.answer:
(a) Break-even volume where revenue = total cost:
Small: 550*Q = 8M + 250*Q -> 300Q = 8M -> Q = 26,667 visits
Large: 550*Q = 14M + 180*Q -> 370Q = 14M -> Q = 37,838 visits

(b) Indifference volume:
8M + 250Q = 14M + 180Q
70Q = 6M
Q = 85,714 visits

(c) Decision tree (visits capped at facility capacity):

Small (cap 40,000): profit = 4M at each demand level (capacity limits)
EMV(small) = 4M

Large (cap 80,000):
- 50K demand: profit = 27.5M - 23M = 4.5M
- 65K demand: profit = 35.75M - 25.7M = 10.05M
- 85K demand (visits capped at 80K): profit = 44M - 28.4M = 15.6M
EMV(large) = 0.4*4.5 + 0.35*10.05 + 0.25*15.6 = 9.22M

Large facility has EMV of $9.22M vs. small's $4M. Choose large.

(d) Qualitative factors:
1. Cost of unmet demand: lost ED visits can mean patients die or suffer worse outcomes.
2. Reputation and legal risk: turning away patients creates malpractice risk.
3. Strategic position: ED is the front door to the hospital.
4. Variability: actual day-to-day demand will vary much more than averages.
5. Construction risk: big capital commitment and long construction period.
6. Workforce: staffing the larger ED is a separate constraint.
7. Reversibility: neither facility can be easily downsized.

Recommendation: EMV and public health both favor large. Build large.

rubric.key: Correct break-even calculations
Correct indifference volume calculation
Correct decision tree with capacity constraints and EMV
Thoughtful qualitative factors beyond EMV

questions.question: An auto parts manufacturer uses 18,000 brackets per year in its assembly operation. Ordering cost from the supplier is $45 per order; annual holding cost is $3 per bracket. Lead time is 6 working days, with 250 working days per year. Daily demand has a standard deviation of 8 brackets. (a) Compute EOQ and the corresponding number of orders per year. (b) Compute the reorder point for a 97% service level (z=1.88). (c) Annual holding and ordering costs at the EOQ. (d) The supplier offers a 2% discount on the $25 unit cost if the manufacturer orders in quantities of 1,500 or more. Holding cost is 12% of unit cost. Should the manufacturer accept?
questions.answer:
(a) EOQ = sqrt(2*18000*45/3) = sqrt(540,000) = 734.85 ~ 735 units
Number of orders per year = 18,000/735 = 24.49 orders

(b) Daily demand = 72 units/day. Lead time = 6 days.
Expected demand during lead time = 432 units
sigma_DL = 8 * sqrt(6) = 19.60
Safety stock = 1.88 * 19.60 = 36.84 ~ 37 units
ROP = 432 + 37 = 469 units

(c) Annual costs at EOQ:
Ordering cost = 24.49*45 = $1,102.04
Holding cost = (735/2)*3 = $1,102.50
Total = $2,204.54

At optimum EOQ, ordering cost equals holding cost (confirming calculation).

(d) Quantity discount analysis:
With discount: unit cost = $24.50, H_discount = 0.12*24.50 = $2.94/unit/year

EOQ at discounted H: sqrt(2*18000*45/2.94) = 742 units, but discount requires Q >= 1500. Use Q = 1500.

Total cost at Q = 1500 with discount:
- Purchasing = 18000 * $24.50 = $441,000
- Ordering = 12 * $45 = $540
- Holding = 750 * 2.94 = $2,205
- Total = $443,745

Total cost at Q = 735 without discount:
- Purchasing = 18000 * $25 = $450,000
- Ordering + holding = $2,204.54
- Total = $452,204.54

Difference = $8,459.54 savings per year with discount.

Recommendation: ACCEPT the discount.

rubric.key: Correct EOQ and number of orders calculations
Correct safety stock and reorder point
Correct annual cost calculations at EOQ
Correct quantity discount analysis with total cost comparison

questions.question: A Japanese-style pull system uses kanban cards to authorize production. Downstream work cell uses parts at 200 units per hour. The container size is 20 parts. Lead time (production + transit) for a container is 15 minutes = 0.25 hours. Safety factor (alpha) = 0.10. (a) Compute the number of kanban cards needed. (b) Explain what happens if you add extra kanban cards 'just to be safe.' (c) The firm is considering reducing container size from 20 to 10 units. How does this affect kanban count and what are the operational trade-offs? (d) Contrast this pull system with an MRP-based push system for the same situation.
questions.answer:
(a) N = D * L * (1 + alpha) / C = 200 * 0.25 * 1.10 / 20 = 55/20 = 2.75 -> 3 cards

(b) Effect of extra kanban cards:
Adding more cards increases WIP inventory proportionally. Extra cards:
1. Hide problems: machine unreliability or supplier slowness gets masked
2. Increase carrying cost and working capital
3. Lengthen effective lead time (Little's Law)
4. Delay detection of quality issues

Lean practice is to continuously reduce kanban counts until problems surface, then fix the problems (the 'lower the water to expose the rocks' metaphor).

(c) Reducing container from 20 to 10:
N = 200 * 0.25 * 1.10 / 10 = 5.5 -> 6 cards

Trade-offs:
- More frequent changeovers (requires SMED to make feasible)
- Tighter flow, faster problem detection
- Same total WIP in units (6*10 = 60 = 3*20) 
- Smoother flow downstream
- Aligns better with lean principles

(d) Pull vs. MRP push contrast:

Kanban Pull:
- Actual downstream consumption pulls upstream production
- Self-limiting: no overproduction
- Simple, visual, decentralized
- Requires stable demand and short lead times
- Cannot handle very high variety efficiently

MRP Push:
- Master schedule and BOM explosion generate planned orders
- Handles high variety and long lead times
- Can anticipate demand changes weeks in advance
- Requires accurate BOMs, inventory, lead times
- Prone to overproduction
- Bullwhip effect upstream

Hybrid systems are common: MRP for long-lead-time procurement, kanban pull for short-lead-time execution.

rubric.key: Correct kanban calculation
Explanation of why extra kanbans are harmful
Correct recalculation with smaller container and trade-off discussion
Clear contrast between pull and push

questions.question: A value stream mapping exercise at a medical device company revealed the following for a surgical instrument product family: total lead time 28 days, of which value-added time is approximately 5 hours. Process steps with cycle times and wait times: receive materials (0.5 hours VA, 3 days wait), machining (1.5 hours VA, 5 days wait), heat treatment (1 hour VA, 6 days wait), polishing (0.5 hours VA, 4 days wait), inspection (0.5 hours VA, 2 days wait), sterilization (0.5 hours VA, 6 days wait), ship (0.5 hours VA, 2 days wait). (a) Compute process cycle efficiency (PCE). (b) Identify which wastes (the 8 wastes) are most prominent. (c) Prioritize three specific kaizen events to implement first, with expected benefit and reasoning.
questions.answer:
(a) PCE = VA time / total lead time
Using 5 hours VA and 28 days * 8 working hours/day = 224 working hours:
PCE = 5 / 224 = 2.2%

This is very low - almost all time is non-value-added waiting. Typical of most pre-lean operations (1-5%).

(b) Prominent wastes (TIMWOODS):

1. WAITING (most severe): 28 days wait vs. 5 hours work. Largest category.
2. INVENTORY: Long lead time means substantial WIP by Little's Law.
3. TRANSPORTATION: Moving parts between 7 process steps.
4. OVERPRODUCTION: Long queues imply push production creating queues.
5. DEFECTS: Inspection step implies defects being found/reworked.

(c) Three prioritized kaizen events:

1. Reduce wait time between machining and heat treatment via FIFO sequencing and pull signal.
   Expected benefit: 3-4 day lead time reduction.
   Why first: largest single wait in the process.

2. Cellular layout for machining + polishing + inspection.
   Create U-shaped cell with minimal queuing; eliminates ~7 days of wait.
   Expected benefit: 6-7 day lead time reduction, better quality feedback.
   Why second: proven lean intervention with large impact.

3. Source-based quality (poka-yoke and operator inspection).
   Build quality in at machining; inspection becomes verification.
   Expected benefit: 2 days wait reduced; defect rate improvements.
   Why third: longer payback, requires engineering effort.

Collectively could reduce lead time from 28 to 10-12 days in a year.

rubric.key: Correct PCE calculation with interpretation
Multiple waste types with specific evidence
Three specific, prioritized kaizen events with expected benefits
Reasoning for priority order

questions.question: A product X has the following bill of materials: X = 1 A + 2 B; A = 2 C + 1 D; B = 1 C + 3 E. Lead times: X (1 week), A (2 weeks), B (1 week), C (2 weeks), D (3 weeks), E (1 week). Lot-for-lot sizing throughout. Demand: 100 units of X are needed in week 8. On-hand inventory: X=0, A=20, B=50, C=30, D=10, E=100. No scheduled receipts. (a) Compute the gross, net, and planned order releases for each item with the correct time phasing. (b) Identify any feasibility problems given the current week is week 1. (c) What changes would you recommend if feasibility is not met?
questions.answer:
(a) MRP explosion:

Level 0: X
- Gross req: 100 in week 8
- Net req: 100 in week 8 (on hand 0)
- Planned order release: 100 in week 7 (LT 1)

Level 1: A (1 per X)
- Gross req: 100 in week 7
- Net req: 80 (on hand 20)
- Planned order release: 80 in week 5 (LT 2)

Level 1: B (2 per X)
- Gross req: 200 in week 7
- Net req: 150 (on hand 50)
- Planned order release: 150 in week 6 (LT 1)

Level 2: C (2 per A, 1 per B)
- Gross from A: 160 in week 5
- Gross from B: 150 in week 6
- Net week 5: 130 (on hand 30), release 130 in week 3
- Net week 6: 150, release 150 in week 4

Level 2: D (1 per A)
- Gross req: 80 in week 5
- Net req: 70 (on hand 10)
- Planned order release: 70 in week 2 (LT 3)

Level 2: E (3 per B)
- Gross req: 450 in week 6
- Net req: 350 (on hand 100)
- Planned order release: 350 in week 5 (LT 1)

(b) Feasibility check (currently week 1):
- D week 2 release: tight but feasible if action taken immediately
- C week 3 release: feasible (2 weeks out)
- All other releases are further out

All planned order releases are feasible if we act now. D is the tightest.

(c) Recommendations:
- Release D order immediately to meet 3-week lead time
- Monitor long-lead items C and D closely
- Review why on-hand of D is so low vs. need
- Consider safety stock for long-lead critical components
- Work with suppliers to reduce lead time

rubric.key: Correct BOM explosion and time phasing for all levels
Correct identification of feasibility issues
Practical recommendations for risk mitigation

questions.question: Six jobs must be scheduled on a single machine. Job (processing time in days, due date in days from now): J1 (4, 8), J2 (2, 3), J3 (6, 12), J4 (3, 15), J5 (5, 10), J6 (1, 7). (a) Sequence using SPT and compute average flow time, average tardiness, and number of tardy jobs. (b) Sequence using EDD and compute the same measures. (c) Which rule is better for this problem and under what objective?
questions.answer:
(a) SPT order: J6 (1), J2 (2), J4 (3), J1 (4), J5 (5), J3 (6)

Flow times: 1, 3, 6, 10, 15, 21
Total = 56; Average = 9.33 days

Lateness (flow - due):
- J6 due 7, flow 1: -6
- J2 due 3, flow 3: 0
- J4 due 15, flow 6: -9
- J1 due 8, flow 10: +2
- J5 due 10, flow 15: +5
- J3 due 12, flow 21: +9

Average tardiness = (0+0+0+2+5+9)/6 = 2.67 days
Max tardiness = 9 days
Number of tardy jobs = 3

(b) EDD order: J2 (3), J6 (7), J1 (8), J5 (10), J3 (12), J4 (15)

Flow times: 2, 3, 7, 12, 18, 21
Total = 63; Average = 10.5 days

Lateness:
- J2: -1
- J6: -4
- J1: -1
- J5: +2
- J3: +6
- J4: +6

Average tardiness = (0+0+0+2+6+6)/6 = 2.33 days
Max tardiness = 6 days
Number of tardy jobs = 3

(c) Comparison:

Measure | SPT | EDD
Avg flow | 9.33 | 10.50
Avg tardiness | 2.67 | 2.33
Max tardiness | 9 | 6
# tardy | 3 | 3

- SPT wins on average flow time (provably optimal)
- EDD wins on maximum tardiness (provably optimal)
- EDD also wins on average tardiness here

Which rule is better depends on objective:
- Minimizing WIP or flow time: SPT
- Avoiding large late penalties: EDD
- Most job shops with customer commitments: EDD is the better default

rubric.key: Correct SPT sequence and calculations
Correct EDD sequence and calculations
Correct comparison across measures
Reasoned recommendation based on objective

questions.question: A product launch project has the following activities: A (start, 3 weeks), B (after A, 5 weeks), C (after A, 4 weeks), D (after B and C, 6 weeks), E (after C, 3 weeks), F (after D and E, 2 weeks). Each activity's PERT times (a, m, b): A (2, 3, 4), B (3, 5, 7), C (2, 4, 5), D (4, 6, 10), E (2, 3, 5), F (1, 2, 3). (a) Compute expected duration of each activity. (b) Identify the critical path and expected project duration. (c) Compute the probability of finishing within 18 weeks.
questions.answer:
(a) t = (a + 4m + b)/6; v = ((b-a)/6)^2

- A: t = 3.00; v = 0.111
- B: t = 5.00; v = 0.444
- C: t = 3.83; v = 0.25
- D: t = 6.33; v = 1.00
- E: t = 3.17; v = 0.25
- F: t = 2.00; v = 0.111

(b) Paths:
- A-B-D-F: 3.00 + 5.00 + 6.33 + 2.00 = 16.33 weeks
- A-C-D-F: 3.00 + 3.83 + 6.33 + 2.00 = 15.17 weeks
- A-C-E-F: 3.00 + 3.83 + 3.17 + 2.00 = 12.00 weeks

Critical path: A-B-D-F, expected duration 16.33 weeks.

(c) Probability of finishing within 18 weeks:

Variance of critical path = 0.111 + 0.444 + 1.00 + 0.111 = 1.666
sigma = sqrt(1.666) = 1.291

Z = (18 - 16.33)/1.291 = 1.293

P(Z < 1.29) ~ 0.901 = 90.1%

Probability ~ 90% of finishing within 18 weeks.

Caution: the second path (A-C-D-F at 15.17 weeks) is close to the critical path. Under high variability it could become critical. Monte Carlo simulation would give a more accurate answer.

rubric.key: Correct expected durations and variances
Correct critical path identification
Correct probability calculation
Recognition of near-critical path caveat

questions.question: A distribution company is choosing among three potential warehouse locations. Factor weights and scores (1-100): proximity to market (0.30) A:90, B:70, C:60; labor availability (0.20) A:65, B:85, C:75; transportation costs (0.25) A:70, B:80, C:90; incentives (0.15) A:50, B:75, C:80; infrastructure (0.10) A:85, B:80, C:70. (a) Compute the factor rating score for each location. (b) The CEO argues that transportation cost should be weighted 0.40 instead of 0.25, with the extra 0.15 coming equally from proximity and incentives. Recompute. (c) Does the choice change? What does this reveal about factor rating methods?
questions.answer:
(a) Original factor rating:

A: 0.30*90 + 0.20*65 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*85
= 27.0 + 13.0 + 17.5 + 7.5 + 8.5 = 73.5

B: 0.30*70 + 0.20*85 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*80
= 21.0 + 17.0 + 20.0 + 11.25 + 8.0 = 77.25

C: 0.30*60 + 0.20*75 + 0.25*90 + 0.15*80 + 0.10*70
= 18.0 + 15.0 + 22.5 + 12.0 + 7.0 = 74.5

Ranking: B (77.25) > C (74.5) > A (73.5). Choose B.

(b) New weights: proximity 0.225, labor 0.20, transport 0.40, incentives 0.075, infrastructure 0.10.

A: 0.225*90 + 0.20*65 + 0.40*70 + 0.075*50 + 0.10*85
= 20.25 + 13.0 + 28.0 + 3.75 + 8.5 = 73.5

B: 0.225*70 + 0.20*85 + 0.40*80 + 0.075*75 + 0.10*80
= 15.75 + 17.0 + 32.0 + 5.625 + 8.0 = 78.375

C: 0.225*60 + 0.20*75 + 0.40*90 + 0.075*80 + 0.10*70
= 13.5 + 15.0 + 36.0 + 6.0 + 7.0 = 77.5

Ranking: B (78.375) > C (77.5) > A (73.5). Still choose B.

(c) Insights:

1. Robustness: B remains dominant under both weightings. Sensitivity analysis should always be part of factor rating.

2. Ranking stability: the gap between B and C narrowed from 2.75 to 0.875, suggesting close ranking. Probably within noise of subjective scoring.

3. Weighting bias: reweighting did not flip the ranking because C's weakness in proximity and labor still offset its transport advantage.

4. Subjectivity: both weights and scores are subjective. Factor rating structures the debate, it does not eliminate it.

5. Practical recommendation: supplement factor rating with cost modeling, site visits, and scenario analysis when rankings are close.

rubric.key: Correct original calculations
Correct recomputation
Ranking stability analysis and insight

questions.question: A call center has one agent. Calls arrive at 20 per hour (Poisson). Average handling time is 2.5 minutes (exponential). (a) Compute utilization, average number of callers in the system, average time in the system, and average time in queue. (b) Management wants average wait time under 90 seconds. Compute the minimum number of agents needed. (c) Beyond adding agents, identify three operational improvements that could reduce wait time without additional staff. (d) Why does adding one agent at 95% utilization have a bigger effect than adding one agent at 70% utilization?
questions.answer:
(a) M/M/1 analysis:
Lambda = 20 calls/hour, Mu = 60/2.5 = 24 calls/hour
Rho = 20/24 = 0.833 = 83.3%

L = rho/(1-rho) = 5.0 callers
W = 1/(mu-lambda) = 0.25 hours = 15 minutes
Lq = rho^2/(1-rho) = 4.17 callers
Wq = 0.208 hours = 12.5 minutes

Average caller waits 12.5 minutes before getting an agent, total 15 minutes in system. Way above 90-second target.

(b) Try c=2: rho = 20/48 = 0.417. Using M/M/c formulas:
With c=2, lambda/mu = 0.833:
P0 = 1/[1 + 0.833 + 0.694/(2*0.583)] = 1/2.428 = 0.412
Lq = (0.412 * 0.694 * 0.417)/(2 * 0.340) = 0.175
Wq = 0.175/20 = 0.00876 hours = 31.5 seconds

With c=2, Wq = 31.5 seconds, well under 90 seconds. Minimum = 2 agents.

(c) Operational improvements without adding staff:

1. Reduce handling time: training, better knowledge base, better CRM tools.
2. Smooth arrivals: callback scheduling, self-service IVR.
3. Deflect calls via self-service: FAQ, chatbot, IVR.
4. Prioritization: put high-value calls ahead.
5. Reduce variability: consistent scripts reduce service time variance.

(d) Impact of utilization:

At 70% utilization: wait times are already modest, adding an agent brings modest improvement.

At 95% utilization: wait times are enormous (Wq grows as 1/(1-rho)). Adding an agent cuts wait from catastrophic to acceptable, a much bigger improvement.

Fundamental 'capacity vs. waiting' curve: waiting time is linear in utilization up to ~70%, then climbs, then explodes as utilization approaches 100%. Marginal value of capacity is highest at high utilization.

This is why targeting 70-85% utilization is standard for most service operations.

rubric.key: Correct M/M/1 calculations
Correct M/M/2 analysis
Three operational improvements
Correct utilization explanation

questions.question: A mid-size appliance manufacturer faces the following situation: high product variety (40+ SKUs), moderate volumes, seasonal demand (60% of sales in March-August), aging facilities with rising maintenance costs, 15% defect rate from its largest component supplier, and a 28-day finished goods lead time vs. 7-day customer expectation. Recommend a coordinated improvement program touching at least four operations domains covered in this course. For each recommendation, state the expected impact and the expected risks.
questions.answer:
Coordinated improvement program:

1. QUALITY AND SUPPLY CHAIN - Address 15% supplier defect rate.
Action: Supplier development program, audit, joint SPC, quality engineer embedded at supplier. Qualify alternate supplier if needed.
Impact: Reducing defect rate 15% to 3% cuts scrap, rework, and delays; eliminates 2-3 weeks of disrupted lead time.
Risks: Supplier relationship damage; alternate qualification takes 3-6 months.

2. INVENTORY AND FORECASTING - Manage seasonal demand.
Action: Winter's method forecasting; seasonal inventory smoothing; produce ahead of peak.
Impact: Smooths production, avoids peak crunches.
Risks: Higher inventory cost; forecast errors still possible.

3. PROCESS AND LEAN - Reduce 28-day lead time to 7 days.
Action: Value stream map; 5S; cellular manufacturing; SMED; pull production with kanban; postponement strategy.
Impact: 60-80% lead time reduction typical in first year; would match customer expectations.
Risks: Lean transformations fail without leadership commitment; multi-year investment.

4. CAPACITY AND FACILITIES - Address aging facilities.
Action: Strategic capacity review; refurbish or replace equipment; decision tree with scenarios.
Impact: Modern facilities enable lean flow; poor facilities cap other improvements.
Risks: High capital cost; payback multi-year.

5. OPERATIONS STRATEGY - Provide strategic coherence.
Action: Clarify competitive priorities; reduce variety if not aligned; use Fisher's framework.
Impact: Aligns all decisions around coherent strategy.
Risks: Choosing wrong position; internal resistance to focusing portfolio.

Sequencing: Quality/supplier first (immediate pain), then lean/process (fastest tangible improvements), then inventory/forecasting, then capacity/facilities. Strategic clarity underlies all.

Executed over 18-24 months with discipline, could move firm from crisis to competitive.

rubric.key: Addresses four or more operations domains
Each recommendation has expected impact with magnitude
Each recommendation identifies concrete risks
Sequencing logic showing understanding of dependencies

major_assignments.title: Operations Strategy Analysis of a Public Company

Identify its competitive priorities using order-winner/order-qualifier framing, diagnose how its structural and infrastructural operations decisions support (or fail to support) its strategy, evaluate its Hayes-Wheelwright stage, and recommend two specific operational improvements with quantitative impact estimates.
